OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 46-year-old male patient, with a known history of
 
 human immunodeficiency virus
 
 and
 
 hepatitis C virus
 
 coinfection, underwent a computed tomography (CT) to assess liver lesions detected by US, which were later identified as haemangiomas. On physical examination, hypoplastic fingernails were found.
 Imaging Findings: Incidentally, the CT revealed the presence of bilateral osseous processes projecting from the posterior aspect of the iliac bones (Figures 1a and 1b). Anteroposterior and lateral knee radiographs showed bilateral patellar hypoplasia associated with luxation (Figure 2a), while elbow radiographs demonstrated bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature (Figure 2b).
PostDescription:

Anteroposterior and lateral knee radiographs showed bilateral patellar hypoplasia associated with luxation, while elbow radiographs demonstrated bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Nail–patella syndrome, Coffin–Siris syndrome, Meier–Gorlin syndrome, Coxopodopatellar syndrome, Genitopatellar syndrome

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Bilateral patellar hypoplasia with dislocation (bacterial luxation).  
- Bilateral hypoplasia of the radial heads with radiohumeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature.  
- Bilateral osseous processes from the posterior iliac bones (could represent iliac horns).  
- Hypoplastic fingernails on clinical examination.  
- Patient is a 46-year-old male with HIV/HCV coinfection, but these do not directly explain musculoskeletal findings.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Nail–patella syndrome:  
   Pros: Classic findings include nail hypoplasia (especially fingernails), patellar hypoplasia or aplasia with possible dislocation, and iliac horns (bilateral posterior iliac spine processes). Radial head hypoplasia and radiohumeral dislocation can also be present but are less common. The combination of nail findings and skeletal abnormalities is hallmark.  
   Cons: None significant based on the given findings.  

2) Coffin–Siris syndrome:  
   Pros: May show nail hypoplasia, but primarily distal phalangeal hypoplasia and often hypertrichosis and developmental delay.  
   Cons: Does not typically present with patellar hypoplasia, iliac horns, or radial head hypoplasia with dislocations.  

3) Meier–Gorlin syndrome:  
   Pros: May include short stature and patellar anomalies.  
   Cons: Primarily characterized by microtia, patellar aplasia, and short stature; does not typically present with radial and elbow problems or bilateral iliac horns. Fingernail hypoplasia is not a typical feature.  

4) Coxopodopatellar syndrome:  
   Pros: Has features of severe nail dystrophy and patellar anomalies.  
   Cons: Usually includes more complex limb anomalies and often intellectual disability; bilateral iliac horns are not characteristic, and radial head involvement is not typical.  

5) Genitopatellar syndrome:  
   Pros: Includes patellar hypoplasia and radioulnar dislocation.  
   Cons: Typically includes genitourinary anomalies and intellectual disability. Iliac horns are not characteristic; no mention of rib or vertebral anomalies in this case. Nail hypoplasia is not a prominent feature.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Nail–patella syndrome (NPS) - Matches all key findings perfectly (nail hypoplasia, bilateral iliac horns, patellar hypoplasia/dislocation).  
2) Coxopodopatellar syndrome - Some overlap, but less typical.  
3) Genitopatellar syndrome - Overlap but lacks nail hypoplasia and iliac horns.  
4) Meier–Gorin syndrome - Limited overlap.  
5) Coffin–Siris syndrome - Least consistent with skeletal findings.  

This leaves Nail–patella syndrome as the most consistent diagnosis.  

</analysis><final> Nail–patella syndrome </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old male with a history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation presents to the emergency department with a one-week onset of orthopnoea and exertional dyspnoea. Laboratory tests demonstrate elevated troponins, the electrocardiogram reveals a third-degree atrioventricular block, and the echocardiogram shows right ventricular thickening and pericardial effusion.
 Imaging Findings: A PET-CT scan (Figure 1) was carried out revealing a large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue. It extended through the right chambers and around the large vessels, sparing the left ventricle. Additionally, a hypermetabolic nodular image was identified at the left lung base.
 
 Therefore, a contrast-enhanced chest CT scan was performed (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c and 2d). It confirmed the presence of an infiltrative hypodense mass in the myocardium. It involved the right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, and atrioventricular groove. The mass caused mural thickening of the right cavities. A cuff encircled the right coronary artery along its course. A moderate pericardial effusion was also observed. Finally, the CT scan confirmed the solid nodule in the posterior segment of the left lower lobe.
 
 A CT-guided percutaneous biopsy of the lung nodule was made for histological examination.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old male with a history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation presents to the emergency department with a one-week onset of orthopnoea and exertional dyspnoea. Laboratory tests demonstrate elevated troponins, the electrocardiogram reveals a third-degree atrioventricular block, and the echocardiogram shows right ventricular thickening and pericardial effusion.
 Imaging Findings: A PET-CT scan was carried out revealing a large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue. It extended through the right chambers and around the large vessels, sparing the left ventricle. Additionally, a hypermetabolic nodular image was identified at the left lung base. Therefore, a contrast-enhanced chest CT scan was performed. It confirmed the presence of an infiltrative hypodense mass in the myocardium. It involved the right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, and atrioventricular groove. The mass caused mural thickening of the right cavities. A cuff encircled the right coronary artery along its course. A moderate pericardial effusion was also observed. Finally, the CT scan confirmed the solid nodule in the posterior segment of the left lower lobe. A CT-guided percutaneous biopsy of the lung nodule was made for histological examination.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Angiosarcoma, Ig-G4 related disease, Primary cardiac lymphoma, Erdheim–Chester disease, Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- Elderly male with paroxysmal atrial fibrillation presenting with orthopnoea and exertional dyspnoea.
- Elevated troponins, third-degree AV block, echocardiogram with right ventricular thickening and pericardial effusion.
- PET-CT: large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue involving right chambers and large vessels, sparing the left ventricle. Hypermetabolic nodule in left lung base.
- Contrast-enhanced CT chest: infiltrative hypodense mass in myocardium involving right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, atrioventricular groove; mural thickening of right chambers; cuff around right coronary artery; moderate pericardial effusion.
- Lung nodule confirmed as solid.
- CT-guided biopsy of lung nodule performed for histology (not provided here).

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:

1. Angiosarcoma:
   Pros: Typically involves right atrium predominately; can cause infiltrative mass; hypermetabolic on PET; can involve large vessels; can cause pericardial effusion.
   Cons: Usually primary cardiac tumour; lung metastasis less common; absence of nodular lung mass less typical but possible; no mention of hemorrhage typical for angiosarcoma.

2. Ig-G4 related disease:
   Pros: Can cause infiltrative myocardial disease and mass-like lesions; may cause pericardial effusion; can affect large vessels.
   Cons: Usually has more diffuse involvement, often bilateral and symmetric; often not hypermetabolic on PET; usually involves other organ systems; lung involvement is common but typically as nodules or interstitial disease, not mass.

3. Primary cardiac lymphoma:
   Pros: Typically involves right side of heart; can present with right chamber involvement; hypermetabolic on PET; could present with pericardial effusion; lung involvement possible either primary or metastatic.
   Cons: Lung nodule could be metastasis or synchronous lymphoma; patient age and presentation fit; typically in immunocompromised but can occur in immunocompetent.

4. Erdheim–Chester disease (ECD):
   Pros: Rare xanthomatous non-Langerhans cell histiocytosis; involves long bones and retroperitoneum classically; cardiac involvement common but usually around great vessels or right atrium; can cause infiltration around coronary arteries; pericardial effusion possible.
   Cons: Typically older patients; lung involvement possible but often nodules; myocardial infiltration can be diffuse, usually perivascular involvement rather than bulky mass; lack of bone lesions mentioned (could be omitted).

5. Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma (UPS) (formerly malignant fibrous histiocytoma):
   Pros: Rare primary cardiac tumor, usually left atrium (but can involve right chambers); hypermetabolic on PET; infiltrative mass; can cause pericardial effusion; lung metastases common.
   Cons: Location often left side; right atrial/ventricular involvement less common; no bone involvement.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:
1. Primary cardiac lymphoma: right heart involvement predominates; hypermetabolic infiltrative mass; lung nodule may represent metastasis or synchronous lymphoma; pericardial effusion; consistent with typical presentation.
2. Angiosarcoma: right atrium preferential involvement and infiltrative mass fits PET and CT; however, lung nodule could be metastasis but angiosarcoma usually primary cardiac and rare lung metastasis.
3. Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma: possible but right heart location less typical.
4. Erdheim–Chester disease: some fitting features, but no bone disease described.
5. Ig-G4 related disease: less likely due to PET hypermetabolism and mass-like myocardial infiltration.

Given the overall right heart predominant infiltrative hypermetabolic mass, pericardial effusion, AV block, elevated troponins, and synchronous lung nodule suitable for biopsy (likely lymphoma), Primary cardiac lymphoma is most consistent.

</analysis>

<final>
Primary cardiac lymphoma
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 37-year-old man presented to our department of surgery with complains of high-grade fever, chills, vomiting and abdominal pain in epigastric region for the last 7 days. Laboratory investigations revealed a raised total leukocyte count (TLC) (of 18,500 cells/μL) with increased absolute neutrophil count (80%), and mildly deranged alkaline phosphate levels (200 IU/L).
 Imaging Findings: Initial abdominal ultrasonography (USG) revealed hepatomegaly with a large left lobe liver abscess (350 cc) and signs of impending rupture. Mild prominence of intrahepatic bile ducts (Figures 1a and 1b) and a few intrahepatic veno-venous collaterals (measuring ~2 mm in diameter) were also noted between the branch of the middle and right hepatic vein (Figures 2a, 2b and 6).
 
 Doppler study showed echogenic content with absent colour flow at the porto-splenic confluence, suggesting thrombosis, along with collateral vessel formation at the porta hepatis, likely indicative of cavernous transformation (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, 4a and 4b).
 
 Contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) scan of the abdomen confirmed the abscess and thrombosis, additionally revealing pinpoint narrowing of the inferior vena cava (IVC) with a small segment thrombus (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, 5d and 5e). Notably, pancreatic parenchymal varices were observed (involving the head, and body of the pancreas) draining directly into the splenic vein (Figures 3e and 4c).
 
 Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) was also performed (Figures 7 and 8), revealing prominent intrahepatic bile ducts in the left lobe of the liver and non-visualisation of the biliary channels distal to the common hepatic duct (CHD) consistent with biliary obstruction.

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 37-year-old man with high-grade fever, chills, vomiting, and epigastric pain for 7 days.  
- Lab: WBC 18,500 with 80% neutrophils, mildly raised alkaline phosphatase (200 IU/L).  
- USG: Hepatomegaly with a large left lobe liver abscess (350 cc), impending rupture. Intrahepatic bile duct prominence, intrahepatic veno-venous collaterals.  
- Doppler US: Echogenic content with absent flow at porto-splenic confluence → thrombosis; cavernous transformation at porta hepatis.  
- CECT abdomen: Confirms liver abscess and portal vein thrombosis; pinpoint narrowing of IVC with a small thrombus segment. Pancreatic parenchymal varices in head and body draining into splenic vein.  
- MRCP: Prominent intrahepatic bile ducts in left lobe; non-visualisation of bile ducts distal to CHD → biliary obstruction.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Liver cirrhosis with portal hypertension and varices formation  
   - Pros: Varices present; portal hypertension suggested by collaterals.  
   - Cons: No mention of liver surface nodularity or typical cirrhotic changes; presence of a large hepatic abscess and acute thrombosis makes cirrhosis less likely as the primary diagnosis.  

2) Arteriovenous malformation (AVM)  
   - Pros: Intrahepatic veno-venous collaterals seen, which could be misread as AVMs.  
   - Cons: No evidence of arterial phase enhancement typical of AVM; clinical picture and thrombosis not typical for AVM; liver abscess presence less likely related to AVM.  

3) Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess  
   - Pros: Portal vein thrombosis (pylephlebitis) present, consistent with infection and abscess. Pancreatic parenchymal varices are described and draining into splenic vein, consistent with portal hypertension secondary to portal vein thrombosis. The liver abscess is the likely source of infection/thrombosis causing pylephlebitis with downstream complications.  
   - Cons: Pancreatic varices rare but explained by portal hypertension from pylephlebitis.  

4) Cystic lesions of pancreas  
   - Pros: Pancreatic changes noted.  
   - Cons: No cystic pancreatic lesions described; varices, not cysts. Pancreas is not the primary site of lesion.  

5) Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms  
   - Pros: Pancreatic duct irregularities could hint at IPMN.  
   - Cons: No signs of mucinous cystic neoplasm; no pancreatic duct dilation or typical IPMN imaging features; main pathology is hepatic abscess and portal vein thrombosis.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
- Top candidate: Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess. This best fits the imaging findings of portal vein thrombosis (pylephlebitis) with subsequent cavernous transformation, pancreatic parenchymal varices due to resultant portal hypertension, and the liver abscess as the source of infection and thrombosis.  
- Second: Liver cirrhosis with portal hypertension and varices formation – less likely due to lack of cirrhotic imaging findings and acute infection being primary.  
- Others ruled out due to lack of supportive imaging features.  

</analysis><final>  
Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 31-year-old male presented at the urologist with scrotal swelling for 4 weeks. He had no relevant history. Clinically there was a voluminous solid swelling of the right hemiscrotum and retraction of the penis. Blood results showed extremely high alpha-fetoprotein levels >53.000 µg/L.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularisation (Figures 1, 2 and 3). There was no visualisation of normal testicular parenchyma or epididymis on the right. There was a normal left testis and epididymis (not shown).
 
 CT showed, besides the large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum (Figure 4), no metastasis or lymphadenopathies.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 31-year-old male presented at the urologist with scrotal swelling for 4 weeks. He had no relevant history. Clinically there was a voluminous solid swelling of the right hemiscrotum and retraction of the penis. Blood results showed extremely high alpha-fetoprotein levels >53.000 µg/L.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularisation. There was no visualisation of normal testicular parenchyma or epididymis on the right. There was a normal left testis and epididymis (not shown). CT showed, besides the large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum, no metastasis or lymphadenopathies.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Mixed germinal cell tumour, Pure yolk sac tumour, Seminoma, Choriocarcinoma, Teratoma, Lymphoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- Patient: 31-year-old male.
- Clinical presentation: Scrotal swelling for 4 weeks, voluminous solid swelling of the right hemiscrotum with penile retraction.
- Blood results: Extremely high alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) levels >53,000 µg/L.
- Imaging: Ultrasound shows a large heterogeneous mass with solid and cystic/necrotic components, vascularisation, absence of normal right testicular/parenchyma and epididymis. CT shows no metastasis or lymphadenopathy. Left testis is normal.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Mixed germinal cell tumour:
   - Pros: Mixed GCTs can have elevated AFP levels and present as large heterogeneous scrotal masses.
   - Cons: No evidence of mixed components like choriocarcinoma or seminoma; extremely high AFP levels are more specific for yolk sac components.

2) Pure yolk sac tumour:
   - Pros: Yolk sac tumour is the only testicular germ cell tumour that is characteristically associated with extremely high AFP levels. The large heterogeneous mass fits the description. The absence of other tumour components or metastasis aligns with this.
   - Cons: Less common in adults (though possible), but the very high AFP is a strong point.

3) Seminoma:
   - Pros: Common testicular tumour.
   - Cons: Seminomas typically do not elevate AFP. AFP is usually normal or slightly elevated in some rare cases, but not >53,000 µg/L. Also, seminomas usually present as homogeneous masses.

4) Choriocarcinoma:
   - Pros: Can produce elevated AFP sometimes, but more commonly elevated ß-hCG.
   - Cons: AFP is not typically markedly elevated in choriocarcinoma. Imaging typically shows hemorrhagic necrosis and early metastasis; none seen here.

5) Teratoma:
   - Pros: Can have heterogeneous mass with cystic/solid components.
   - Cons: AFP typically not markedly elevated. Usually mixed with other germ cell components; no such evidence here.

6) Lymphoma:
   - Pros: Can present as testicular mass.
   - Cons: Typically doesn't elevate AFP. Usually homogeneous and uniform without cystic necrotic components. No evidence of lymphoma-related findings here.

C) Ranking:
1) Pure yolk sac tumour
2) Mixed germinal cell tumour
3) Seminoma

Given the pathognomonic extremely high AFP level and imaging findings, pure yolk sac tumour is the most likely diagnosis.

</analysis><final>Pure yolk sac tumour</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 40-year-old man with lower abdominal pain received a CT abdomen/pelvis in the emergency department to rule out appendicitis. An incidental fat attenuation mass was present in the right ischiorectal fossa. Five years later, the patient presented again with pelvic pain and received a CT pelvis.
 Imaging Findings: The initial CT study identified a right ischiorectal fossa fat attenuation mass displacing the anal canal towards the left without enhancing soft tissue, fluid or calcifications (Figures 1a and 1b). There was a thin capsule surrounding the mass.
 
 When the patient presented again five years later, a CT pelvis showed that the mass had significantly enlarged (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). It contained two well-defined cystic compartments with homogeneous fluid attenuation (15 HU) and an irregular enhancing soft tissue rim in the posterosuperior compartment.
 
 An MR pelvis done shortly thereafter recapitulated these findings but revealed that the apparently homogeneous central collection on CT contained a mix of fluid and soft tissue, with both thin and thick septations (Figures 3a, 3b, 4a, and 4b). Additionally, the entire mass had a thick, irregular rim of enhancement.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old female patient was admitted to our oncology centre with follicular lymphoma. She had a seven-kilogram weight loss over one year, loss of appetite, and fever of unknown origin. There is no significant medical history, and physical examination revealed only cervical lymphadenopathy.
 
 The patient’s blood workup revealed a slight increase in C-reactive protein (1.2 mg/dL) and creatine kinase (151 U/L), with no other abnormal laboratory findings.
 Imaging Findings: Computed tomography detected a large lesion, measuring 7.5 x 5 centimetres, heterogeneous, with fluid densities and lobulated margins in the superior segments of the right hepatic lobe (Figure 1). Due to the
 
 patient’s
 
 clinical context, an ultrasound-guided biopsy was performed, which revealed an unrepresentative fragment of liver tissue, encompassing a fibro-hyalinised nodule.
 
 An abdominal magnetic resonance (MR) was performed to characterise the hepatic lesion further (Figures 2, 3a, 3b, and 4). The MR revealed a heterogeneous, complex cystic lesion with a thin wall, hypointense on T2-weighted images, and hyperintense on T1-weighted images, located within the right liver lobe. The lesion exhibited radiating septa towards the periphery, appearing as low-signal intensity on T2-weighted images and high-signal intensity on T1-weighted images. These septa delineated multiple smaller cysts, hypointense on T1 and hyperintense on T2-weighted images, occupying almost the entire lesion. After contrast administration, the lesion did not exhibit restricted diffusion and no significant enhancement of the soft tissue component.

Clinical History: A one-year-old full-term-born male child, born to non-consanguineous parents, presents with complaints of thoracic deformity. No history of previous surgeries.

 Further evaluation with computed tomography revealed bifid ribs with segmental fusion and widening at multiple levels bilaterally (Videos and Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 3, 4, and 5).
 
 Fusion of posterior vertebral elements was observed at multiple levels, and dextroscoliosis was noted involving the thoracic spine. Posterior vertebral arch defects were identified involving the L5 vertebra and all sacral vertebrae (Figures 2b and 2c).
 
 Mild volume loss of the right hemithorax was noted. The pulmonary parenchyma, bronchial, and vascular markings appeared normal bilaterally.

- On AP chest radiograph: absence of ribs in right mid-zone and fusion of multiple adjacent ribs on both sides (right and left).  
- Evidence of dextroscoliosis (rightward curvature of the thoracic spine).  
- CT shows bifid ribs with segmental fusion and widening at multiple levels bilaterally.

1) Spondylocostal dysostosis (Jarcho–Levin syndrome):  
   - Pros: Associated with segmentation abnormalities leading to rib and vertebral anomalies; presence of rib anomalies and vertebral fusions suggest this.  
   - Cons: Usually associated with multiple rib anomalies and thoracic insufficiency, but presence of bifid ribs and fusion is more characteristic of spondylothoracic dysostosis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 17-month-old boy presented with a 3-day history of sternal swelling and pain. No fever or any
 
 other
 
 symptoms. There was no history of trauma. Physical examination revealed a 2 cm swelling in the manubriosternal region. Inflammatory parameters were normal except for
 
 a slightly elevated value for C-reactive protein.
 Imaging Findings: The chest radiograph revealed a presternal soft tissue swelling without calcifications and with no evidence of bony anomalies at the sternum, such as bone erosions or a periosteal reaction (Figure 1).
 
 A CT scan showed a fusiform soft tissue density lesion, centred on the manubriosternal joint, involving both superficial and deep planes of the synchondrosis, with subcutaneous and mediastinal extension, measuring 46 x 27 x 37 mm. No significant irregularity of the bony surfaces at the manubriosternal synchondrosis is visible.
 
 However, a separation between the manubrial and sternal ossification centres is evident
 
 , accompanied by densification of the adjacent subcutaneous tissue (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 Ultrasound revealed a hypoechoic and heterogeneous, dumbbell-shaped lesion centred on the manubriosternal synchondrosis. This lesion crosses the synchondrosis, extending superficially into the subcutaneous tissue and deeply to the depth of the chest wall/anterior mediastinum. Colour Doppler ultrasound demonstrated no internal vascularisation. Additionally, there is a thickening of the soft tissues surrounding the manubriosternal joint (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c].
 
 Bone scintigraphy showed no significant abnormalities.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A newborn with ambiguous genitalia and multiple congenital anomalies was admitted in March 2023. Born preterm at 34 weeks, the infant faced respiratory distress, electrolyte imbalances, and sepsis.
 Imaging Findings: The patient presented with extensive congenital anomalies, including an omphalocele with liver and bowel herniating through a membrane-covered defect, bladder exstrophy, and imperforate anus.
 
 Spinal imaging revealed fusion defects in the lower thoracic and lumbar vertebrae, with an associated cystic mass with dystrophic neural elements. Cranial imaging showed a low-lying torcular Herophili, ventriculomegaly, fullness of the foramen magnum, and possible aqueductal stenosis. Other imaging findings included ectopic kidneys (one partially thoracic and another pelvic), midgut malrotation, sacral agenesis, rib synostosis, pubic symphysis diastasis, ambiguous genitalia, and other musculoskeletal abnormalities, highlighting extensive multi-system involvement.
 
 Due to the patient’s medical instability and eventual demise, as well as lack of resources, further imaging could not assess other abnormalities including undescended testes and detailed evaluation of the urinary system. These structural abnormalities posed considerable diagnostic and management challenges.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 85-year-old woman with no significant past medical history presented with a rapidly growing mass on her left shoulder (Figure 1), with no history of trauma.
 
 On physical examination, the mass was hard and non-mobile.
 Imaging Findings: A shoulder X-ray and ultrasound scan were performed in order to rule out malignancy.
 
 The X-ray (Figure 2) shows a rounded, homogeneous soft-tissue density mass on the left shoulder, located just above the acromioclavicular joint (ACJ).  On the US exam (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c), the images show a supraclavicular fluid collection, predominantly anechogenic but heterogeneous, that appears to be erupting superiorly from the acromioclavicular (AC) interval. No Doppler signal is detected. On interrogation of the rotator cuff, the exam shows the absence of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons over the humeral head, as well as bony contour irregularity.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 71-year-old lady reported to the emergency with a history of a fall and was referred for CT brain. Retrospective history revealed recurrent swelling and itching over the left temporal region for the last 6 months.
 Imaging Findings: No trauma-related abnormality was seen on CT. However, a thick-walled cystic lesion was noted in the subcutaneous plane on the left temporal region of the scalp (Figure 1).
 
 Ultrasound of the lesion showed a cyst with actively mobile, tubular serpiginous structures, having echogenic walls showing
 
 a “filarial dance”
 
 sign (Figures 2 and 3).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 71-year-old lady reported to the emergency with a history of a fall and was referred for CT brain. Retrospective history revealed recurrent swelling and itching over the left temporal region for the last 6 months.
 Imaging Findings: No trauma-related abnormality was seen on CT. However, a thick-walled cystic lesion was noted in the subcutaneous plane on the left temporal region of the scalp. Ultrasound of the lesion showed a cyst with actively mobile, tubular serpiginous structures, having echogenic walls showing a “filarial dance” sign.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Dirofilariasis, Epidermal inclusion cyst, Cysticercosis, Foreign body granuloma

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
1) Patient is a 71-year-old lady with history of fall but no trauma-related abnormalities on CT.  
2) Incidentally, a thick-walled cystic lesion is found in the subcutaneous plane of the left temporal scalp region.  
3) Ultrasound shows a cyst containing actively mobile, tubular serpiginous structures with echogenic walls.  
4) The described "filarial dance" sign is classic for motile filarial larvae in a cyst.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1) Dirofilariasis:  
   - Pros: Presence of thick-walled cystic subcutaneous lesion with motile tubular structures on ultrasound is characteristic. The "filarial dance" sign is highly suspicious for dirofilariasis. Patient's history includes itching and swelling for 6 months, consistent with chronic subcutaneous infection.  
   - Cons: Dirofilariasis is rare but endemic in some areas; depends on location and exposure. No direct history of mosquito bites mentioned but not required.  

2) Epidermal inclusion cyst:  
   - Pros: Can present as a thick-walled cystic lesion in the subcutaneous tissue; typical for scalp region.  
   - Cons: Ultrasound would usually show a well-defined anechoic or mixed echogenic cyst without internal mobile structures. No motile serpiginous structures; no "filarial dance," so less likely.  

3) Cysticercosis:  
   - Pros: Can cause cystic lesions; sometimes subcutaneous.  
   - Cons: Cysticercosis usually involves brain parenchymal cysts or subcutaneous nodules but rarely shows "filarial dance" sign. Ultrasound would show cyst with scolex, not motile serpiginous structures.  

4) Foreign body granuloma:  
   - Pros: May present as a cystic lesion in subcutaneous tissue and cause swelling.  
   - Cons: No internal motile tubular structures on ultrasound; no filarial dance sign; no history of foreign body introduction.  

C) Ranking and decision:  
1) Dirofilariasis (Pathognomonic "filarial dance" sign, consistent with ultrasound findings and clinical presentation)  
2) Epidermal inclusion cyst (less likely lacks motile structures)  
3) Cysticercosis (ultrasound and clinical features not consistent)  
4) Foreign body granuloma (no supporting history or imaging findings)  

Given the ultrasound finding of actively motile tubular serpiginous structures with echogenic walls causing a "filarial dance" sign, the best diagnosis is Dirofilariasis.  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Dirofilariasis  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 18-year-old female presented to the maxillofacial surgeon with persistent primary teeth and a palpable left pre-auricular mass, which she first noticed two years prior. No other symptoms were present.
 Imaging Findings: An
 
 orthopantomogram
 
 showed a large exophytic sclerotic bone lesion in the left mandible ramus, persistent primary teeth, caries, and multiple smaller sclerotic bone lesions interspersed in the mandible, some with cortical expansion (Figure 1). A CT scan of the maxillofacial region confirmed the presence of a
 
 dense ivory-like exophytic lesion growing out of the left mandibular ramus
 
 (Figure 2), compatible with an osteoma, and multiple well-delineated lesions of mature bone between the roots of the teeth compatible with hypercementosis.
 
 During clinical examination, a mass was palpated on the forehead leading to a CT scan of the skull, revealing multiple small, sessile sclerotic lesions protruding from the outer table (Figure 3), also consistent with osteomas.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A nine-year-old female child presented with fever, restriction of neck movements and posterior bulging of the mucosa of the posterior oropharyngeal/hypopharyngeal wall lateralised to the left side on nasopharyngolaryngoscopy. Piriform sinuses were inspected and did not show any irregularities.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the neck revealed a well-defined oval fluid collection, with rim enhancement, in the retropharyngeal space, measuring 2 x 2 x 1 cm
 
 (ap/axial/cc) (
 
 rrows in Figures 1
 
 and
 
 2). Retropharyngeal space oedema was also present, from the skull base to the hypopharynx level (
 
 small arrow in Figure 2
 
 ). Additionally, a thin well-defined rim-
 
 enhancing
 
 fistulous tract could be identified, running from the inferior left border of the retropharyngeal collection to the upper pole of the left thyroid lobe (arrows on Figure 3).

 Imaging Findings: CT showed diffuse enlargement of the left parotid gland with increased heterogeneous uptake of contrast material, fat stranding in the left parotid space, and thickening of the adjacent superficial layer of the deep cervical fascia (Figures 1 and 3). Oedema and fat stranding extended slightly into the soft tissues of the ipsilateral buccal and masticator spaces, and the sternocleidomastoid muscle was enlarged (Figures 2 and 4). There was no involvement of deeper compartments.
 
 No haematomas or signs of active bleeding were observed.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-day-old male presented with non-bilious emesis and a left thigh cutaneous lesion. The patient was found to have pyloric atresia requiring gastroduodenostomy; this procedure led to intestinal malrotation, and the appendix was removed prophylactically. During surgery, the cutaneous lesion was also biopsied. The patient was transiently jaundiced following surgery; this resolved spontaneously, but a liver ultrasound was requested as part of the workup of this.
 Imaging Findings: Liver US showed a normal biliary tree and 2 hypoechoic liver lesions: the larger a 6 mm lesion in segment IVb (lesion 1). Contrast-enhanced ultrasound (CEUS) was performed to further characterise. Three lesions were examined with 4 boluses of ultrasound (Total vol. 1.1mL, Optison, GE Healthcare Inc., using a Phillips EPIQ machine). Lesion 1 had increased in size (seg. IVb, now 32 mm) (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d); Lesions 2 (seg. II, 7 mm) (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d) and 3 were also seen (seg. I,10 mm) (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d).
 
 To further characterise, a liver MRI with hepatobiliary contrast was performed. Lesion 1 (34 mm) was T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, infiltrative, rectangular area with capsular retraction (Figures 4a, 4b and 4c). Lesions 2 (Figure 4d) and 3 were T1 hypointense with central T2 hyperintensity relative to the liver. No lesions showed enhancement, macroscopic fat, or diffusion restriction.

- Hepatoblastoma: 
  Pros: Common primary malignant liver tumor in neonates and infants; can present as solitary or multiple liver lesions; usually hyperechoic or heterogeneous; may have hypovascularity on imaging. 
  Cons: Typically shows heterogeneous enhancement; often associated with AFP elevation (not mentioned here); tends to have central necrosis with possible hemorrhage; capsular retraction is less common. The absence of enhancement on MRI and no diffusion restriction is unusual for hepatoblastoma.

  Pros: Can involve liver and cause multifocal lesions; lymphoma commonly shows diffusion restriction due to high cellularity; lesions often hypoechoic on US. 
  Cons: Lesions here have no diffusion restriction, making lymphoma less likely. Also, no mention of systemic lymphadenopathy or bone marrow involvement. 

- Infantile myofibromatosis:

Also, MRI typical findings for myofibromatosis show T1 hypointensity and T2 heterogeneity. 
  Cons: None significant; fits better than other candidates.

- Neuroblastoma: 
  Pros: Frequent in young children; can metastasize to liver. Usually presents with primary adrenal or abdominal mass and liver metastases. Lesions often hypervascular. 
  Cons: No adrenal mass reported; no enhancement typical for metastases; absence of typical neuroblastoma clinical picture; no mention of elevated catecholamines or primary tumor. Less likely here.

- Haemangiomata: 
  Pros: Common benign liver tumors in infants; appear as well-defined, homogenous hyperechoic lesions on US; tend to show hyperenhancement on contrast imaging; typically hyperintense on T2-weighted MRI.

Cons: Typically no capsular retraction; lesions usually show enhancement on CEUS and MRI, which is not seen here; absence of typical progressive centripetal enhancement pattern.

C) Ranking:  
1) Infantile myofibromatosis – best fits the combination of cutaneous lesion (thigh), liver hypoechoic lesions without enhancement or diffusion restriction, capsular retraction, and MRI features.  
2) Hepatoblastoma – less likely due to lack of enhancement and atypical MRI features.  
3) Lymphoma – less likely due to no diffusion restriction and lack of systemic signs.  
4) Neuroblastoma – unlikely without primary tumor or typical metastatic features.  
5) Haemangiomata – least likely given lack of enhancement and capsular retraction.

<final>Infantile myofibromatosis</final><|return|>
options: Hepatoblastoma | Lymphoma | Infantile myofibromatosis | Neuroblastoma | Haemangiomata

 o’clock; m
 
 oreover, there is a similar mass in the left axillary region.

 The histopathologic exam reveals highly atypical spindle cells with large pleomorphic hyperchromatic nuclei and high nucleo/cytoplasmic ratio (Figure 4).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 19-year-old male presented to the orthopaedic surgeon with complaints of recurrent left shoulder pain. The patient practices combat sports and associates the complaints with a recent fall during training. A shoulder magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was conducted, followed by MRI arthrography (MRA) due to inconsistent findings.
 Imaging Findings: MRI revealed oedematous changes in the inferior glenohumeral joint capsule, with increased signal intensity on the fat-saturated proton-density (PDFS) sequence, suggestive of an
 
 inferior glenohumeral ligament
 
 (
 
 IGHL) lesion (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c).
 
 Additionally, there was an increased signal intensity in the superior labrum, which may indicate a labral lesion.
 
 However, this finding was considered non-specific due to the absence of intra-articular fluid or contrast (Figure 1a).
 
 Subsequent MRA revealed an intrasubstance full-thickness rupture of the anterior band of the IGHL with contrast extravasation (Figures 2a and 2b). This finding was not documented on the previous conventional MRI, which only indicated capsular oedema.
 
 Additionally, the axillary pouch changed from its normal U-shape to a J-shape on fat-saturated T2-weighted sequences (Figures 3a and 3b),
 
 indicating IGHL laxity and loss of its stabilising function
 
 .
 
 MRA also identified a
 
 superior labrum from anterior to posterior (
 
 SLAP) lesion (Figures 2c, 2d, and 3c), which was not clearly visible on MRI due to lack of fluid joint distention.
PostDescription:

This finding was not documented on the previous conventional MRI, which only indicated capsular oedema. OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old man presented to the emergency department with tibial and fibular fractures and a traumatic brain injury (TBI) after a motor vehicle accident.
 Imaging Findings: Imaging studies obtained after the trauma revealed a comminuted wedge fracture of the tibial diaphysis with mild displacement and angulation, along with an overlapping fibular fracture (Figure 1), and multifocal subarachnoid haemorrhage in the right frontoparietal and left parietal regions on brain CT (Figure 2).
 
 Six months after the initial injury, during which the patient remained bedridden without any new trauma history, shoulder X-rays and CT scans were performed due to complaints of shoulder pain (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). The imaging revealed extensive bilateral heterotopic ossification in the lower glenohumeral joint, indicating mature ossification.

The Achilles tendons are thickened and hypoechogenic. The findings were identical in both Achilles tendons.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old athlete with chest discomfort and palpitations presented to the cardiologist. Electrocardiogram showed low-density ventricular extrasystoles at rest, which increased in density with stress. Echocardiography revealed mild mitral regurgitation and mild left ventricular hypertrophy
 
 with a basal septal thickness of 12 mm.
 Imaging Findings: Cardiac magnetic resonance was performed on a 3 Tesla MRI scanner. Steady-state free precession (SSFP) sequences identify billowing of both mitral leaflets, with mitral valve prolapse (MVP) of the posterior leaflet (5 mm), mitral annular disjunction (6 mm) and “curling” (Figures 1a and 1b; yellow arrow). Mitral regurgitation was not significant. Biventricular function was normal.
 
 Late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) was present in the subendocardial–intramyocardial layer of the basal inferolateral wall, suggesting replacement fibrosis (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d; white arrows). Native T1 mapping in the same region was elevated: 1360 ms (local normal range 1150–1250 ms), confirming myocardial fibrosis (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d; yellow arrow). Cardiac CT ruled out significant stenosis in the circumflex artery and posterolateral artery (Figure 3; circumflex artery: yellow arrow).

Clinical History: A 4-month-old male infant presented with a palpable mass on his arm. The parents noticed it since he was a month and a half old. Clinical examination showed a painless mass without signs of inflammation.
 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound (US), the mass was poorly defined and hyperechoic compared to muscle, located in the subcutaneous tissue. The lesion exhibited a serpentine pattern of hypoechoic bands with a hyperechoic border [1] (Figure 1a).
 
 It showed low vascularity on colour Doppler (Figure 1b).
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a non-encapsulated soft tissue mass in the subcutaneous tissue of the arm, resting on the fascia without involving the underlying muscle. T1 and T1 fat-saturated images showed isointense tissue relative to muscle, with interspersed fatty foci (Figures 2a and 2b). No enhancement was noted after gadolinium injection (Figure 2c).

- Ultrasound: poorly defined, hyperechoic compared to muscle, subcutaneous location, serpentine hypoechoic bands with hyperechoic border, low vascularity on Doppler.

- Cons: None significant.

- Cons: Rhabdomyosarcoma often shows aggressive features like muscle infiltration, heterogeneous signal intensity, possible necrosis, and typically hypervascularity on Doppler. MRI would show enhancement post-gadolinium, not seen here. Location may be subcutaneous but infiltration typical.

- Pros: Common pediatric tumor presenting as superficial mass with fatty components.

- Cons: Extremely rare in infants, usually in adults. Malignancy is rare in children, and liposarcomas are usually deep-seated, large, infiltrative, and show enhancement and heterogeneity. Not fitting the benign appearance and age.

- Pros: Common benign tumor, often subcutaneous.  
- Cons: Ultrasound and MRI usually show homogeneous fatty mass, well-defined capsule, and isointense/hyperintense on T1 with fat suppression showing fatty signal.  procedure that was performed under fluoroscopy guidance and intravenous deep sedation. Because the graft was in the right iliac fossa
 
 , t
 
 he patient was supine.
 
 The antegrade pyelography revealed a stricture at the ureteral-bladder anastomosis and a
 
 tortuosity of the ureter
 
 , causing an occlusive narrowing of the ureter that prevented the contrast medium from passing into the bladder.
 
 Despite several attempts through multiple angles of the C-arm, from 18° cranial (CRA) to 34° caudal (CAU) and from 52° right (RAO) to 19° left (LAO) angulations, the ureteric bud cannot be visualised

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 24-year-old lady presented to the emergency department with accelerated hypertension. She had a history of recurrent episodes of sudden onset palpitations, headache and giddiness for the past two years, with uncontrolled hypertension despite being on multiple antihypertensive medications. Her blood investigations revealed elevated serum normetanephrine (1990 pg/mL).
 Imaging Findings: Trans-abdominal ultrasound (Figures 1a and 1b) showed a well-defined heterogeneously hyperechoic lesion in the right adnexa, seen separate from the right ovary, measuring approximately 4.7 x 5 x 4.8 cm, and with high internal vascularity.
 
 CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis (Figures 2a, 2b, 3a, and 3b) showed a hyperdense mass (average HU of 29) with intense heterogeneous arterial enhancement and few central non-enhancing areas suggestive of necrosis. It was located in the right adnexa and paracervical region, abutting the uterus (which was displaced to the left). It was abutting the posterior urinary bladder wall anteriorly and the rectum posteriorly with no invasion of the right ureter, iliac vessels and lateral pelvic side wall.
 
 On MRI of the pelvis, the lesion was deep to the lateral pelvic fascia on the right, heterogeneously iso to hyperintense on T1WI (Figures 4b and 5), heterogeneously hyperintense on T2WI (
 
 “light bulb sign”
 
 ) (Figure 4a), with areas of restricted diffusion on DWI and maintained fat planes with the adjacent structures (Figures 6a and 6b).
 
 On 68Ga-DOTATATE scan, there was intense homogeneous SSTR (somatostatin receptor) expression in a large well-defined soft tissue density mass in the right adnexa and paracervical region with maximum standardised uptake value (SUV max) of 20.19 (Figure 7).

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Clinical presentation strongly suggestive of catecholamine-producing tumor: accelerated hypertension, episodes of palpitations, headache, giddiness, and markedly elevated serum normetanephrine.  
- Imaging findings: a well-defined, heterogeneously hypervascular lesion in the right adnexa/paracervical region.  
- CT: hyperdense mass with intense heterogeneous arterial enhancement and central necrosis, no invasion of adjacent critical structures.  
- MRI: heterogeneously iso to hyperintense on T1, heterogeneously hyperintense on T2 (described as "light bulb sign", which is typical of highly vascular lesions such as paraganglioma/pheochromocytoma), restricted diffusion areas, and maintained fat planes.  
- 68Ga-DOTATATE PET scan: intense homogeneous somatostatin receptor expression with SUV max of 20.19, typical for neuroendocrine tumors like paragangliomas.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Solitary fibrous tumour (SFT):  
   - Pros: SFT can occur anywhere and is a hypervascular soft tissue lesion.  
   - Cons: SFT does not typically elevate serum normetanephrine; lesions are not neuroendocrine tumors; lack of catecholamine-related clinical symptoms; DOTATATE scan not typically positive.  

2) Pelvic paraganglioma:  
   - Pros: Highly consistent with catecholamine excess symptoms and markedly elevated normetanephrine; imaging matches typical hypervascular lesion with intense arterial enhancement and high T2 signal ("light bulb sign"); DOTATATE avidity is classic for neuroendocrine tumors including paragangliomas.  
   - Cons: None significant.  

3) Hypervascular soft tissue sarcoma:  
   - Pros: Hypervascular, destructive potential.  
   - Cons: No elevated catecholamines; clinical history does not fit; DOTATATE negative typically; no invasive features described.  

4) Hypervascular lymphadenopathy (metastases or Castleman disease):  
   - Pros: Hypervascular lesions can be seen in Castleman's disease and nodal metastases.  
   - Cons: Serum normetanephrine rise not explained; clinical symptoms not typical; lesion is well-defined mass not typical for lymphadenopathy; no evidence of widespread disease.  

5) Peripheral nerve sheath tumour (PNST):  
   - Pros: Can be heterogeneously enhancing.  
   - Cons: No catecholamine excess; no clinical features of neuroendocrine tumor; no DOTATATE avidity; mass is not typical in location or enhancement pattern for PNST.  

6) Aggressive angiomyxoma:  
   - Pros: Rare pelvic myxoid tumor in women.  
   - Cons: Typically large, infiltrative, with characteristic myxoid matrix and different imaging features (T2 hyperintensity but not typically hyperdense or hypervascular with intense enhancement); no catecholamine excess; DOTATATE negative.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Pelvic paraganglioma (top candidate due to congruent clinical, biochemical, and imaging findings).  
2) Others are less likely due to mismatch in clinical, biochemical, or imaging features.  

Justification: The combination of accelerated hypertension with markedly elevated serum normetanephrine, intense hypervascular lesion with characteristic "light bulb" T2 hyperintensity, and strong DOTATATE avidity all point to the diagnosis of pelvic paraganglioma.  
</analysis>

<final>Pelvic paraganglioma</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old woman diagnosed with recurrent meningioma located in the foramen magnum consulted for headache and paraesthesia of the upper limbs. It was decided to perform decompressive surgery and, in the immediate postoperative period, she presented tetraplegia with preservation of consciousness and requirement of mechanical ventilation.
 Imaging Findings: The preoperative MRI showed an extra-axial expansive lesion located in the foramen magnum, measuring 3.9 x 2.1 cm, and producing a mass effect on the bulb and spinal cord, displacing them posteriorly and to the left. It also compromised the V4 segment of the right vertebral artery (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d).
 
 The MRI on day +2 after the surgery showed signs of tumour persistence of the partially removed lesion (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c) and signal alteration in the bulb and upper spinal cord, with hyperintensity in T2/FLAIR sequences (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c) and hypointensity in T1. Diffusion sequences showed signs of mixed oedema (vasogenic-cytotoxic), all in relation to ischemic-oedematous phenomena (Figures 4a to 4i).

It also compromised the V4 segment of the right vertebral artery. The MRI on day +2 after the surgery showed signs of tumour persistence of the partially removed lesion and signal alteration in the bulb and upper spinal cord, with hyperintensity in T2/FLAIR sequences and hypointensity in T1. Diffusion sequences showed signs of mixed oedema (vasogenic-cytotoxic), all in relation to ischemic-oedematous phenomena.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 63-year-old woman with known recurrent foramen magnum meningioma undergoing decompressive surgery.  
- Postoperative tetraplegia with preserved consciousness, requiring mechanical ventilation.  
- MRI shows residual tumor and signal abnormalities in the bulb and cervical spinal cord (hyperintense on T2/FLAIR, hypointense on T1).  
- Diffusion imaging indicates mixed vasogenic and cytotoxic edema suggesting ischemic and/or oedematous changes.  
- Surgical complication appears to be acute neurological deficit rather than delayed mechanical compression (no mass effect noted on postoperative MRI).  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Central cord syndrome  
- Pros: This syndrome can present with weakness greater in the upper extremities than lower; tetraplegia could be consistent.  
- Cons: Typically due to trauma or spinal cord compression affecting central axons, usually from hyperextension injuries or disc herniation; no mention of such trauma. Imaging would show central cord hyperintensity but no specific ischemic-edematous changes as described.  
2) White cord syndrome  
- Pros: Rare postoperative complication characterized by acute neurological deterioration after cervical decompressive surgery, with hyperintense T2 signal in the cord due to reperfusion injury and edema. Imaging findings of mixed vasogenic and cytotoxic edema with cord signal changes fit this pattern.  
- Cons: Usually occurs after decompression of chronic severe compression, as in this case. No explicit mention of reperfusion injury, but imaging and clinical course are typical.  
3) Compressive myelopathy  
- Pros: Clinical and imaging findings include spinal cord compression and resultant cord edema.  
- Cons: Postoperative MRI already shows decompressed area with no mass effect; tetraplegia occurs postoperatively without evidence of new compression (e.g., no epidural collection).  
4) Epidural haematoma  
- Pros: Postoperative tetraplegia could be caused by epidural hematoma compressing the spinal cord.  
- Cons: No imaging evidence of epidural collection; imaging shows cord signal changes but no mass effect from hemorrhage.  

C) Ranking and justification:  
1) White cord syndrome: Highly consistent with acute postoperative tetraplegia after cervical decompression due to reperfusion and ischemic injury causing cord edema and signal changes, including mixed vasogenic-cytotoxic edema on DWI. This matches the clinical history and imaging.  
2) Central cord syndrome: Less likely due to absence of trauma or typical central canal lesion; imaging findings more ischemic/edematous rather than pure central cord hyperintensity.  
3) Compressive myelopathy: Unlikely given postoperative decompression and no mass effect on imaging.  
4) Epidural haematoma: Ruled out by lack of epidural collection on MRI.  

<final> White cord syndrome </final><|return|>
options: Central cord syndrome | White cord syndrome | Compressive myelopathy | Epidural haematoma

 drug. Neurological examination shows vibratory

 deficit
 
 , proprioceptive impairment in lower limbs, and

 at two locations: in the cervical spine extending from

 , sparing the conus medullaris [1].

- MRI shows symmetrical bilateral T2 hyperintensity confined to posterior columns of cervical spinal cord (C1-C6) and lower thoracic spinal cord, sparing conus medullaris.  
- Lesions are specific to posterior columns (spinocerebellar tracts), affecting both cervical and thoracic levels.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Multiple sclerosis  
   - Pros: MS can involve spinal cord lesions and present with sensory symptoms.  
   - Cons: MS lesions are typically asymmetric, short segmental, and involve multiple white matter areas, not exclusively posterior columns over long segments. Clinical history of nitrous oxide abuse and specific posterior column involvement are atypical. Imaging does not show the typical Dawson's fingers or multiple plaque pattern.  

2) Neurosyphilis  
   - Pros: Can cause myelopathy with dorsal column involvement.  
   - Cons: No history suggestive of syphilis; no other signs or laboratory data; neuroimaging usually shows enhancement and diffuse involvement, not symmetrical long posterior column lesions. Also, time frame of 15 days is acute, less typical for neurosyphilis.  

3) Neurosarcoidosis  
   - Pros: Can involve spinal cord and cause myelopathy.  
   - Cons: Typically involves longitudinally extensive lesions, but often heterogenous and asymmetrical; usually involves multiple levels but not restricted to posterior columns. No systemic sarcoid signs or granulomatous disease mentioned.  

4) HIV-related vacuolar myelopathy  
   - Pros: HIV can cause posterior column myelopathy.  
   - Cons: No history of HIV infection or immunocompromise mentioned; no CD4 count or other clinical clues supportive; MRI usually shows long segments, but in context of HIV infection and systemic signs; nitrous oxide abuse is a more relevant clue here.  

5) Methotrexate-induced myelopathy  
   - Pros: Methotrexate can cause posterior column myelopathy.  
   - Cons: No history of methotrexate use; no mention of chemotherapy or rheumatologic disease; less likely in absence of drug exposure.  

6) Subacute combined degeneration secondary to abuse of nitrous oxide  
   - Pros: Nitrous oxide abuse leads to B12 deficiency or functional deficiency through inactivation of methylcobalamin, causing demyelination of dorsal columns and lateral corticospinal tracts (subacute combined degeneration). Clinical presentation matches (paresthesia, loss of proprioception/vibration at lower limbs, gait ataxia), and MRI findings typically show symmetric T2 hyperintensity in dorsal columns.  
   - Cons: No mention of laboratory B12 levels; however, nitrous oxide abuse is clear and classic for this condition.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Subacute combined degeneration secondary to abuse of nitrous oxide (clear clinical history, typical posterior column involvement on MRI, classic presentation)  
2) Multiple sclerosis (less likely due to symmetric, long posterior column lesions, atypical in this age and exposure context)  
3) Neurosyphilis (clinical history and imaging less supportive)  
4) Neurosarcoidosis (clinical and imaging less consistent)  
5) HIV-related vacuolar myelopathy (no HIV history)  
6) Methotrexate-induced myelopathy (no drug exposure)  

Thus, the most likely diagnosis is Subacute combined degeneration secondary to abuse of nitrous oxide.  
</analysis>  
<final>Subacute combined degeneration secondary to abuse of nitrous oxide</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 29-year-old healthy woman presented with a 4-day history of right breast pain and periareolar bruising associated with a 1-day periareolar indurated tumefaction. She denied any other symptoms, trauma, or regular medication use. Physical examination revealed a 2-cm indurated, painful cord-like structure in the periareolar region associated with bruising and petechial lesions.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasonography of the right breast revealed a subcutaneous, non-compressible, tubular hypoechoic structure with low-level internal echoes along an extension of approximately 28 mm (Figures 1 and 2). No blood flow was detected on colour or spectral Doppler studies (Figure 3). Furthermore, the adjacent soft tissues showed increased echogenicity. No other anomalies were seen in the right breast. Ultrasound scans of the left breast and axillary regions yielded no significant findings.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 53-year-old male patient presented with a lump in his left jaw, with noted enlargement in 2 months. There were no associated symptoms or relevant clinical history. Physical examination disclosed an expansile lesion in the left mandibular region and excluded palpable lymph nodes.
 Imaging Findings: Non-enhanced computed tomography (CT) dental scan showed an expansile lytic lesion centred to the angle and ascending ramus of the left mandible, measuring 4 cm at its largest axis, with well-defined, multilobulated borders and ground-glass ossified matrix (Figures 1a and 1b). The final report included osteosarcoma and chondrosarcoma as diagnostic hypotheses and the patient was referred to our tertiary hospital.
 
 Contrast-enhanced cervical CT performed at our hospital better depicted that the lesion did not invade adjacent structures (Figure 2) and enabled evaluation of the infra-hyoid neck. A solid, hypodense, 3.5 cm mass was identified posterior to the right thyroid lobe (Figure 3) and a provisional diagnosis of parathyroid adenoma was proposed.
 
 On ultrasound, the referred nodule was well-defined and hypoechoic (Figures 4a and 4b).
 
 Laboratory findings revealed elevated parathyroid hormone (PTH) (301pg/mL) and elevated calcium levels (13mg/dL).
 
 Parathyroid scintigraphy with technetium-99m methoxy-isobutyl-isonitrile (
 
 Tc-99m MIBI)
 
 was performed and an adenoma of the right inferior parathyroid was confirmed (Figures 5a and 5b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 62-year-old woman with a 10-year history of limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis presented with insidious pain and swelling of the left thumb’s fingertip. Physical examination revealed sclerodactyly and a tender nodule on the distal phalanx of the first finger.
 Imaging Findings: Radiographic imaging of the left hand (Figures 1a and 1b) and thumb (Figure 2) revealed a well-defined calcification in the soft tissue overlying the distal phalanx of the left first finger, measuring approximately 1.5 cm. Periarticular osteopenia was also observed, particularly evident at the thumb (Figure 1b). Cortical thinning and blurring of the margins were noted in the proximal interphalangeal joint of the ring finger (Figure 1a). No other significant findings were identified.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 58-year-old male with worsening nausea for the past 72 hours presented to the emergency department with severe chest pain radiating to the back in the context of 4 recent episodes of non-bloody, non-bilious emesis. He was afebrile, diaphoretic, and mildly tachycardic. Cardiac workup, complete blood count, and electrolytes were normal.
 Imaging Findings: A chest X-ray followed by contrast-enhanced chest CT were performed. The X-ray demonstrated pneumomediastinum extending into the neck and a tiny left apical pneumothorax. Notably, left pleural effusion with atelectasis and consolidation were also seen (Figure 1a). The coronal CT images were closely correlated to the X-ray findings, clearly showing the pneumomediastinum extending superiorly towards the neck soft tissues. A pneumothorax at the left lower lung base, not previously seen on X-ray, is also demonstrated on coronal CT view (Figure 1b).
 
 The axial chest CT image on lung window showed evidence of pneumomediastinum with air around the oesophagus. Left pneumothorax and bilateral pleural effusions are also seen (Figure 2). On the soft tissue window, a thickened distal oesophagus was seen; however, no clear site of perforation was appreciated. Bilateral complex attenuating pleural effusions were also observed but there was no obvious contrast extravasation (Figure 3).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Case 1 is a 54-year-old female who experienced right ankle pain after an injury, leading to the clinical suspicion of Achilles tendon rupture, and was evaluated by radiograph and MRI after 1 week.
 
 Case 2 is a 62-year-old male who reported calf pain, raising concerns for deep vein thrombosis, further evaluated with ultrasound. He noted a trivial trauma
 
 occurred 10 days prior.
 Imaging Findings: In Case 1, the lateral ankle radiograph revealed increased soft tissue thickness around the Achilles tendon with small calcification (Figure 1). MRI indicated significant disruption (95%) of the distal 5.5 cm of the tendon, cystic areas, and signs of haemorrhage (Figures 2a, 2b, 3a, 3b, and 4). Extensive oedema in the posterior lower leg muscles led to a screening of the leg that identified a 2.8 x 1.6 x 5.4 cm collection in the intermuscular fascial plane, causing indentation on the soleus. The plantaris tendon was partially torn, while the soleus and medial gastrocnemius exhibited grade 1 strain without fibre discontinuity (Figures 5a and 5b).
 
 In Case 2, the ultrasound showed a bulky distal 6.0 cm Achilles tendon with altered echotexture, multiple calcific foci, and a high-grade partial thickness tear (Figures 6a, 6b, and 6c). An anechoic collection measuring 3.3 x 1.2 x 8.6 cm was observed in the intermuscular fascial plane, between the gastrocnemius and soleus muscles, with no increased vascularity noted on the Doppler study (Figures 7a and 7b).

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
1) Clinical history: Both patients present with ankle/calf pain with history of trauma (injury in one case, trivial trauma in the other). Pain after trauma raises suspicion for either tendon rupture or muscle injury.  
2) Imaging: Radiograph in Case 1 shows increased soft tissue thickness around the Achilles tendon with small calcifications. MRI in Case 1 reveals extensive tendon disruption (>90% of distal 5.5 cm tendon), cystic areas, haemorrhage, and extensive oedematous changes in posterior lower leg muscles. Notably, there is a sizable intermuscular Fascial plane collection indenting the soleus. Ultrasound in Case 2 shows bulky distal Achilles tendon (6 cm) with heterogeneous echogenicity, multiple calcifications, high grade partial thickness tear, and a large anechoic collection in the intermuscular fascia between gastrocnemius and soleus. No vascularity is noted on Doppler.  
3) In addition to the tendon findings, both cases have large fluid collections in the intermuscular fascial planes with no vascularity.  
4) Plantaris tendon partially torn in Case 1; no involvement noted in Case 2.  
5) Forensic note: DVT was considered initially in Case 2 but ruled out.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Achilles rupture: Supported by the clinical history of trauma and MRI/ultrasound findings of large tendon tears with haemorrhage and tendon disruption extending over >5.5 cm in Case 1 and similar tendon findings in Case 2. However, Achilles rupture alone would not explain the large fluid collections and intermuscular fascial plane collections.  
2) Tennis leg: Classical for medial gastrocnemius muscle tear causing calf pain and fluid collection in the intermuscular plane; however, both cases show extensive Achilles tendon tears, which are not typical for tennis leg alone.  
3) Achilles rupture with tennis leg: This diagnosis encompasses both Achilles tendon tearing and fluid collections in the intermuscular fascial plane. The large intermuscular fluid collections could be considered "tennis leg," which often occurs secondary to gastrocnemius rupture or other calf muscle injuries. The presence of tendon rupture plus fluid in the fascial plane is consistent with this combined diagnosis.  
4) Ruptured Baker’s cyst: Ruptured Baker's cyst can cause fluid tracking into the lower leg intermuscular fascial planes; however, no signs of Baker’s cyst seen in imaging, and tendon rupture is not characteristic.  
5) Deep vein thrombosis: Initially considered but ruled out with imaging; no signs of venous thrombosis on Doppler or other modalities.

C) Ranking:  
1) Achilles rupture with tennis leg: This diagnosis best encompasses all findings: Achilles tendon tear (supported by MRI and ultrasound), large fluid collections in the intermuscular fascial plane between calf muscles, which fits the definition of tennis leg (usually gastrocnemius or soleus tear with haemorrhagic fluid tracking in fascial planes). The description of calf haemorrhage and fluid collections is consistent.  
2) Achilles rupture: Also supported by tendon findings, but it does not explain the fluid collections in the fascial plane.  
3) Tennis leg: Does not explain the extent of Achilles tendon tear.  
4) Ruptured Baker’s cyst: Inconsistent with imaging and clinical findings.  
5) Deep vein thrombosis: Excluded clinically and with imaging.  

<final>Achilles rupture with tennis leg</final><|return|>
options: Achilles rupture | Tennis leg | Achilles rupture with tennis leg | Ruptured Baker’s cyst | Deep vein thrombosis

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old male, with skin melanoma (IIIc), on adjuvant immunotherapy with nivolumab since 6 months,
 
 previously without comorbidities,
 
 presented with >5-fold increase in serum lipase and
 
 an onset of diabetes
 
 . Nivolumab was suspended till lipase decreased. After the reintroduction of nivolumab, another elevation of lipase
 
 and abdominal pain
 
 were
 
 noted.
 Imaging Findings: Computed tomography showed diffuse pancreatic parenchymal enlargement, heterogeneity (Figure 1), and slightly increased peripancreatic fat attenuation. There were no radiological signs of cholelithiasis or cholecystitis. Additionally, diffuse colon and rectal wall thickening and hyperenhancement were reported (Figure 2), as well as a triangular enhancement defect in the left kidney (Figure 3).
 
 At the second onset of symptoms, MRI was performed, demonstrating pancreatic volume increase, with signal alterations on T2-weighted images, especially in the pancreatic head, and diffuse restricted diffusion (Figures 4a, 4b, 5, 6a, and 6b). Additionally, mildly expressed peripancreatic and mesenteric fat stranding was noted. No pancreatic duct or biliary tree dilatation was observed. There were no fluid collections, free abdominal fluid or adenopathy.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key Findings:  
â¢ Clinical history consistent with immune checkpoint inhibitor (nivolumab) treatment for melanoma, with known adverse effects including endocrine dysfunction and pancreatitis.  
â¢ Laboratory findings of markedly raised lipase and new onset diabetes align with pancreatic inflammation or injury.  
â¢ Imaging shows diffuse pancreatic enlargement, heterogeneity, T2 hyperintensity, restricted diffusion, and mild fat stranding without ductal dilation or fluid collections, suggesting acute and diffuse pancreatic inflammation.  
â¢ Absence of gallstones/cholecystitis rules out biliary pancreatitis.  
âbull Imaging also shows extra-pancreatic findings (colon, rectal wall thickening and hyperenhancement, left renal triangular enhancement defect) indicating possible multi-organ immune-related inflammatory response.  
â¦ B) Candidate pass/fail:  
- Viral pancreatitis: Unlikely, as no viral prodrome or epidemiology; immune therapy timing correlates better.  
- Autoimmune pancreatitis: Could cause diffuse enlargement and delayed enhancement but usually presents with characteristic 'sausage-shaped' pancreas, delayed strictures, typical IgG4-related disease findings, and often a capsule-like rim; no prior IgG4 history or classic imaging features.  
- Idiopathic pancreatitis: Doesn't account for repeated episodes correlated with drug re-introduction; extra-pancreatic inflammation also less expected.  
- Immune-related pancreatitis: Strongly supported by clinical history (nivolumab), timing (post-therapy and reintroduction), laboratory (high lipase, diabetes), imaging (diffuse pancreatic inflammation without ductal features), and extra-pancreatic inflammatory signs.  
- Chronic pancreatitis: No evidence of chronic remodeling (calcifications, ductal changes, atrophy).  
- Normal response to treatment with immune checkpoint inhibitors: Not accurate; the patient has imaging and laboratory evidence of pancreatitis, which is an adverse reaction, not merely a normal response.  
C) Ranking:  
1) Immune-related pancreatitis (best matches clinical and imaging data).  
2) Autoimmune pancreatitis (some overlap but less likely given context).  
3) Viral pancreatitis (less likely due to context).  
4) Idiopathic pancreatitis (less likely due to drug causality).  
5) Chronic pancreatitis (unsupported by imaging).  
6) Normal response (incorrect context).  
</analysis><final>Immune-related pancreatitis</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old male patient on anticoagulation therapy, with a history of hip fracture and acute renal insufficiency, underwent a standard chest X-ray as part of the preoperative evaluation for a total hip replacement. He was asymptomatic on the respiratory level, with no dyspnoea or chest pain.
 Imaging Findings: The initial X-ray revealed a likely pleural right peripheral opacity with smooth borders and no acute angles (Figure 1).
 
 A CT scan was then performed, demonstrating an extra-pleural collection measuring 30 × 72 × 87 mm (AP × TR × CC). The collection was spontaneously hyperdense, suggesting the presence of blood or fluid with a high protein content. Additionally, the “extra-pleural fat sign” was observed, confirming an extra-pleural process rather than a pleural or lung lesion (Figures 2 and 3).
 
 In the presence of renal insufficiency and an extra-pleural mass, contrast injection was not performed. Consequently, a mass with potential bleeding could not be definitively ruled out, and an MRI was conducted. T1-weighted MRI images showed the extra-pleural collection appearing hyperintense due to the presence of blood products (Figure 4). In T2-weighted images, the lesion was mildly hyperintense, consistent with the presence of subacute haemorrhage or an organised haematoma (Figure 5).
 
 On diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI), a hyperintense signal with ADC restriction was observed (Figures 6a and 6b). After gadolinium injection, a peripheral enhancement of the lesion’s capsule was observed, with no enhancement observed within the lesion itself (Figure 7).
 
 This supports the primary haemorrhagic nature of the lesion rather than an inflammatory or neoplastic process.
 
 Non-contrast CT and MRI scans of the thorax were performed using T1, T2, DWI, and T1 fat-saturated gadolinium sequences in a Philips Ingenia 1.5T high-field MRI scanner and a Philips Incisive 32-slice CT scanner.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 81-year-old male with history of anticoagulation therapy and acute renal insufficiency.
- Incidentally discovered right peripheral pleural opacity on chest X-ray.
- CT shows a well-defined extra-pleural collection measuring 30 × 72 × 87 mm, spontaneously hyperdense suggesting blood or high-protein fluid.
- Extra-pleural fat sign present, confirming extra-pleural location.
- MRI T1 hyperintensity indicating blood products, T2 mildly hyperintense consistent with subacute hemorrhage or organized hematoma.
- DWI hyperintensity with ADC restriction.
- Post-gadolinium MRI shows peripheral rim enhancement of capsule, no internal enhancement.
- No clinical respiratory symptoms, no dyspnea or chest pain.
- Patient's anticoagulation and acute renal insufficiency contraindicate contrast CT; MRI done without renal failure concerns.

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:
1) Encysted pleural effusion
   Pros: Location in pleural area, fluid collection.
   Cons: Usually pleural effusions are extrapleural pleural fluid, not true extra-pleural. Extra-pleural fat sign suggests non-pleural location. Also, spontaneous hyperdensity and MR imaging suggest blood rather than simple fluid.

2) Empyema
   Pros: Pleural or extra-pleural collection.
   Cons: Clinical picture lacks symptoms (no fever, no chest pain), no signs of infection or inflammation. MRI shows peripheral enhancement only, no internal enhancement or inflammatory debris. DWI restriction can be seen in abscess, but the absence of clinical signs and lack of internal enhancement argue against empyema.

3) Pleural malignant neoplasm
   Pros: Extra-pleural mass.
   Cons: No abnormal metabolism on imaging; the lesion shows no internal enhancement and there is no nodular or solid mass characteristics. The hyperdense spontaneous attenuation and MR findings consistent with blood argue against a neoplasm. Also, no clinical symptoms. Additionally, the lesion is smooth-bordered, no irregularities.

4) Extra-pleural haematoma
   Pros: Spontaneous hyperdense collection on CT with blood products on MRI (T1 hyperintensity, DWI restriction consistent with subacute blood), peripheral rim enhancement consistent with organized capsule or inflammation around hematoma rather than tumor, presence of extra-pleural fat sign confirms location. History of anticoagulation a major risk factor. Clinical symptoms match an asymptomatic presentation. MRI findings confirm hemorrhagic nature.
   Cons: None significant.

5) Parapneumonic effusion
   Pros: Pleural exudate collection.
   Cons: No clinical infection, no pleuritic pain, no systemic signs. Imaging characteristics not typical (blood products predominance, extra-pleural location). MRI and CT findings more consistent with hematoma than infection.

6) Solitary fibrous tumour
   Pros: Pleural based/extra-pleural soft-tissue mass.
   Cons: Usually intermediate to mildly intense enhancement on MRI with solid appearance; no such internal enhancement in this case. Imaging findings show blood products and hyperdensity on CT suggest hematoma rather than tumour. Clinical history and imaging consistent with hemorrhage, not a tumour.

7) Intercostal nerve tumour
   Pros: Extra-pleural peripheral mass.
   Cons: Usually a small mass rather than a large collection, no blood products. No enhancement characteristics matching tumor. Imaging signs indicate fluid/hyperdense due to blood, not tumor.

C) Ranking:
1) Extra-pleural haematoma - Fits perfectly with spontaneous hyperdense collection on CT due to blood, extra-pleural fat sign confirming location, MRI consistent with blood products (T1 hyperintensity, DWI restriction), peripheral capsule enhancement, anticoagulation history, and no symptoms.
2) Encysted pleural effusion - Does not fit the extra-pleural location or blood characteristics.
3) Empyema - lacks clinical signs of infection and MRI findings do not support empyema.
4) Solitary fibrous tumour - imaging not consistent with solid neoplasm.
5) Pleural malignant neoplasm - no enhancement or malignant features.
6) Parapneumonic effusion - lacks clinical and imaging infection features.
7) Intercostal nerve tumour - not consistent with large haemorrhagic collection.

<final>Extra-pleural haematoma</final><|return|>
options: Encysted pleural effusion | Empyema | Pleural malignant neoplasm | Extra-pleural haematoma | Parapneumonic effusion | Solitary fibrous tumour | Intercostal nerve tumour

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 64-year-old man with a prior history of pulmonary tuberculosis presents with an acute onset of significant haemoptysis. He mentions experiencing weight loss, tiredness, and intermittent fever. His medical history is unremarkable, except for tuberculosis, which was treated a decade ago. A chest X-ray performed recently demonstrated cavitary abnormalities.
 Imaging Findings: CT angiography provided the main findings, consisting of a localised aneurysm of a pulmonary artery branch located near a tuberculous cavity (Figures 2a and 2b), representing the aneurysm. Hyperdense opacity with surrounding haematoma indicates active bleeding, while multiple air locules within the opacity are characteristic of a tuberculous cavity (Figures 1a and 1b), distinguishing it from other pulmonary lesions. Centrilobular ground-glass nodules may be observed around the haematoma, suggesting endobronchial dissemination of blood (Figure 4). Post-contrast imaging reveals focal enhancement of the aneurysm, aiding in precise assessment of its size, shape, and location (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c).
