model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 45-year-old female with primary amenorrhea and monthly cyclic lower abdominal cramps since adolescence.  
- Ultrasound: bulky uterine body, hypoechoic endometrial collection, heterogeneously hypoechoic lesion with internal echogenic debris in right ovary (suggesting hemorrhagic cyst or endometrioma).  
- MRI: fundus and body of uterus present, oriented right lateral; absence of cervix; visualized vagina; endometrial collection within the uterine cavity; T1 hyperintense, T2 mildly hypointense endometrial collection; cystic lesion in right adnexa with T2 shading sign (typical of endometrioma); minimal ascites.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Transverse vaginal septum with obstructed menstruation  
- Pros: cyclic pain and amenorrhea consistent with obstructive lesion; endometrial collection present.  
- Cons: vaginal canal is visualized, implying there is no obstructive septum; uterine anomalies not typically associated; no mention of hematocolpos (blood in vagina).  

2) Imperforate hymen with haematocolpos  
- Pros: amenorrhea and cyclic pain fits classical presentation; endometrial collection present.  
- Cons: vagina is visualized, hymen not mentioned as imperforate; no hematocolpos or accumulation of blood in vagina reported; no mention of distended vagina.  

3) Haemorrhagic ovarian cyst with cervical agenesis  
- Pros: hemorrhagic cyst compatible with endometrioma on MRI; cervical agenesis noted (absence of cervix).  
- Cons: endometrial collection suggests menstrual blood accumulation without egress due to cervical agenesis, causing retrograde symptoms. This seems plausible.  

4) Cervical agenesis with endometrioma  
- Pros: cervical agenesis explains absent cervix and endometrial collection; endometrioma (hemorrhagic cyst) described with T2 shading sign.  
- Cons: same as 3).  

5) Mayer–Rokitansky–Küster–Hauser (MRKH) syndrome  
- Pros: primary amenorrhea is classic; uterine agenesis or malformation.  
- Cons: presence of uterine body and fundus excludes this diagnosis, as MRKH involves uterine agenesis or severe hypoplasia; vagina visualized.  

6) Endometriosis without genital tract abnormality  
- Pros: endometriomas and cyclic pain fit.  
- Cons: presence of cervical agenesis and uterine collection argue for genital tract abnormality. The uterine collection (bloody endometrial fluid) suggests obstructive anomaly, not isolated endometriosis.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Cervical agenesis with endometrioma: fits absence of cervix, endometrial collection (blood retention), adnexal endometrioma (T2 shading sign).  
2) Haemorrhagic ovarian cyst with cervical agenesis: similar to #1, but #2 emphasizes primarily cyst rather than endometrioma. Since T2 shading is classic for endometrioma, #1 is more precise.  
3) Transverse vaginal septum: less likely due to normal-appearing vagina.  
4) Imperforate hymen: less likely due to visualization of vagina and no hematocolpos.  
5) MRKH: excluded due to presence of uterus and vagina.  
6) Endometriosis alone: insufficient to explain cervical agenesis and endometrial collection.  

Thus, the most specific and best fitting diagnosis is "Cervical agenesis with endometrioma."
<final> Cervical agenesis with endometrioma </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 54-year-old man known to have hypertension and type 2 diabetes experienced paraesthesia in his left hand and distal part of his left forearm for several months. On examination, multiple well-defined skin nodules were found, globally distributed, along with reduced sensation distally in the left upper limb.
 Imaging Findings: MRI of the brachial plexus with intravenous contrast showed multiple well-marginated lesions with heterogeneous signal in the infraclavicular part of the left brachial plexus, including the trunks and divisions (Figures 1, 2a, 2b, and 3), extending into the axilla and proximal parts of the upper arm, suggestive of neurofibromas.
 
 MRI of the spine and brain showed no abnormalities, including vestibular schwannoma, and the patient was diagnosed with schwannomatosis.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 54-year-old man known to have hypertension and type 2 diabetes experienced paraesthesia in his left hand and distal part of his left forearm for several months.  Imaging Findings: MRI of the brachial plexus with intravenous contrast showed multiple well-marginated lesions with heterogeneous signal in the infraclavicular part of the left brachial plexus, including the trunks and divisions, extending into the axilla and proximal parts of the upper arm, suggestive of neurofibromas. MRI of the spine and brain showed no abnormalities, including vestibular schwannoma, and the patient was diagnosed with schwannomatosis.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Neurofibromas, Brachial plexus schwannomas, Lymphoma, Brachial plexopathy, Metastases
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 54-year-old male with hypertension and type 2 diabetes.  
- Clinical symptoms: paraesthesia in left hand and distal left forearm for several months.  
- Physical examination: multiple well-defined skin nodules globally distributed; reduced sensation distal in left upper limb.  
- Imaging: MRI of brachial plexus with contrast reveals multiple well-marginated lesions with heterogeneous signal in the infraclavicular part of left brachial plexus, trunks, and divisions, extending into axilla and proximal upper arm. Features suggest neurofibromas.  
- MRI spine and brain normal, no vestibular schwannoma. Diagnosis established as schwannomatosis.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Neurofibromas  
   Pros: Imaging findings exactly describe neurofibromas; clinical exam shows multiple skin nodules, which are consistent with neurofibromas in neurofibromatosis or schwannomatosis.  
   Cons: None significant here.  

2) Brachial plexus schwannomas  
   Pros: schwannomas can involve brachial plexus; they are enhancing nerve sheath tumors.  
   Cons: Lesions described are multiple well-marginated lesions in trunks and divisions, consistent more with neurofibromas, which are often multiple in neurofibromatoses. Schwannomas tend to be solitary and eccentric to the nerve; multiple schwannomas would be expected in bilateral and extensive distribution, but this case shows multiple nodules and known diagnosis of schwannomatosis (association with schwannomas but not neurofibromas?), and classic description is neurofibromas.  

3) Lymphoma  
   Pros: Lymphoma can involve nerves, but is rare.  
   Cons: Lesions typically infiltrative and not well-marginated neurogenic tumors; no systemic lymphoma signs mentioned, no nodal disease. Imaging features not typical for lymphoma.  

4) Brachial plexopathy  
   Pros: Clinical symptom of paraesthesia fits brachial plexopathy.  
   Cons: Imaging shows distinct lesions consistent with tumors, not just inflammatory or obstructive plexopathy without focal tumors. The diagnosis of schwannomatosis with multiple neurofibromatous lesions is more specific.  

5) Metastases  
   Pros: Tumor metastasis can occur to nerve sheaths rarely.  
   Cons: No known primary tumors; lesions well-marginated and nerve-related; metastases usually infiltrative, multiple diffuse lesions, not well-defined neurogenic tumors. Clinical info favors neurogenic tumors.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Neurofibromas (best fit: multiple well-defined lesions in brachial plexus, multiple skin nodules, consistent with neurofibromatosis-spectrum disorder, here schwannomatosis)  
2) Brachial plexus schwannomas (multiple lesions possible in schwannomatosis, but the imaging description is more neurofibroma-like)  
3) Brachial plexopathy (clinical symptoms fit, but imaging shows tumors)  
4) Lymphoma (less likely)  
5) Metastases (least likely)

Thus, best diagnosis: Neurofibromas.  
</analysis><final>Neurofibromas</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 60-year-old female with a known history of chronic kidney disease, on monthly haemodialysis and type 2 diabetes mellitus, presented with confusion and lethargy for the past 2 days. There was no history of trauma, fever, or seizures. On examination, the patient was disoriented but exhibited no focal neurological deficits.
 Imaging Findings: Non-contrast CT (Figure 1) shows bilateral symmetric hypodensity in the lentiform nuclei, with surrounding more hypodense internal and external capsules forming the “lentiform fork sign”. There is a sub-centimetric-sized subcortical calcification in the right parietal lobe.
 
 MRI brain T2-weighted (Figure 2) shows bilateral symmetric hyperintensity in the thalamus, lentiform nuclei, particularly in the putamen and globus pallidus, with marked symmetrical hyperintensity of the internal, external capsules and internal and external medullary laminae.
 
 MRI FLAIR axial section (Figure 3) through the basal ganglia shows symmetric hyperintensity in the external capsule, internal capsule and internal and external medullary laminae on both sides. There is also hyperintensity in bilateral thalamus, putamen and globus pallidi.

MRI FLAIR axial section through the basal ganglia shows symmetric hyperintensity in the external capsule, internal capsule and internal and external medullary laminae on both sides. There is also hyperintensity in bilateral thalamus, putamen and globus pallidi.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>

A) Key findings:
- 60-year-old female with chronic kidney disease on monthly hemodialysis and type 2 diabetes mellitus.
- Clinical presentation: confusion and lethargy, no trauma or fever or seizures, no focal neurological deficits.
- CT: bilateral symmetric hypodensity in lentiform nuclei with "lentiform fork sign," indicating involvement of internal and external capsules.
- MRI T2 and FLAIR: bilateral symmetric hyperintensity in thalamus, lentiform nuclei (especially putamen and globus pallidus), internal and external capsules, and internal and external medullary laminae.
- Presence of subcortical calcification in the right parietal lobe.

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:

1) Uremic encephalopathy
   Pros: Patient has chronic kidney disease on hemodialysis; lentiform fork sign is characteristic of metabolic encephalopathies, especially uremic encephalopathy; bilateral symmetric involvement of basal ganglia and internal/internal capsules aligns with this diagnosis.
   Cons: Hypodense lesions could be caused by other metabolic encephalopathies; but clinical context and imaging fit uremic encephalopathy.

2) Hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy 
   Pros: Can show basal ganglia involvement and hypodensities.
   Cons: No history of hypoxia or ischemic event; patient has no focal deficits; hypoxic-ischaemic changes usually involve watershed areas; lentiform fork sign is not classically described.

3) Hypoglycaemic encephalopathy 
   Pros: Basal ganglia involvement possible.
   Cons: Patient is diabetic but no history of hypoglycemia mentioned; imaging findings like lentiform fork sign and symmetric involvement of medullary laminae and capsules is more typical of uremic encephalopathy.

C) Ranking:
1) Uremic encephalopathy (most consistent with clinical and imaging findings: chronic kidney disease, hemodialysis, lentiform fork sign described classically)
2) Hypoglycaemic encephalopathy (less likely due to lack of hypoglycemia history and typical imaging pattern)
3) Hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy (least likely due to absence of clinical hypoxia/ischemia and characteristic imaging findings)

<final>Uremic encephalopathy</final><|return|>
options: Uremic encephalopathy | Hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy | Hypoglycaemic encephalopathy

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 59-year-old man who had undergone a bilateral vasectomy 20 years earlier underwent a scrotal ultrasound due to mild right testicular discomfort. There was no history of trauma or associated symptoms, and no relevant physical examination or laboratory findings. He had no history of travel outside Europe.
 Imaging Findings: An ultrasound scan using a high-frequency linear transducer (Figures 1a, 1b, and 2) revealed multiple tiny hyperechoic foci within the left epididymal body, measuring less than 1 mm and exhibiting continuous oscillatory movement. The right epididymis had a homogeneous echotexture and normal, symmetric vascularisation using Doppler ultrasound. No abnormal findings were observed in the remaining scrotal ultrasound, including the right testicle and epididymis.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 59-year-old man who had undergone a bilateral vasectomy 20 years earlier underwent a scrotal ultrasound due to mild right testicular discomfort. There was no history of trauma or associated symptoms, and no relevant physical examination or laboratory findings. He had no history of travel outside Europe.
 Imaging Findings: An ultrasound scan using a high-frequency linear transducer revealed multiple tiny hyperechoic foci within the left epididymal body, measuring less than 1 mm and exhibiting continuous oscillatory movement. The right epididymis had a homogeneous echotexture and normal, symmetric vascularisation using Doppler ultrasound. No abnormal findings were observed in the remaining scrotal ultrasound, including the right testicle and epididymis.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Scrotal filariasis, Dancing megasperm, Epididymitis
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 59-year-old male with bilateral vasectomy history.
- Mild right testicular discomfort, no trauma or other symptoms.
- Ultrasound: multiple tiny hyperechoic foci (less than 1 mm) in the left epididymal body with continuous oscillatory movement.
- Right epididymis normal.
- No abnormal findings elsewhere in the scrotum (right testicle and epididymis normal).
- No travel outside Europe history.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Scrotal filariasis:
   Pros: Ultrasonography can show mobile echogenic worms ("filarial dance sign") in filariasis; often presents as hyperechoic moving structures.
   Cons: No travel outside Europe reduces the risk significantly (filariasis is more endemic in tropical/subtropical areas); also, location is left epididymis while symptoms are mild right testicular discomfort; no systemic symptoms.

2) Dancing megasperm:
   Pros: "Dancing megasperm" refers to the ultrasound appearance of viable, motile sperm agglutinated within dilated ducts showing rhythmic motion; usually seen as multiple tiny hyperechoic mobile particles.
   Cons: Typically seen in the epididymal head/duct system; this case is in the left epididymal body; also, the patient has a vasectomy history, which may predispose to spermatocele or obstructive changes but usually no motile sperm are present post-vasectomy.

3) Epididymitis:
   Pros: Can be related to scrotal discomfort and abnormal ultrasound findings.
   Cons: Ultrasound findings in epididymitis usually include enlarged epididymis, increased vascularity, and heterogenous echotexture; not multiple tiny hyperechoic foci with oscillatory movement; no pain or inflammation noted clinically.

C) Ranking:
1) Scrotal filariasis: The "oscillatory movement" and hyperechoic tiny foci in the epididymis with normal testicular findings fit the classic "filarial dance sign." The lack of travel is a downside but not exclusive, since filariasis can occur occasionally in Europe.
2) Dancing megasperm: Less likely because vasectomy would reduce motile sperm presence, and no evidence of sperm aggregates in duct.
3) Epididymitis: Least likely cause due to ultrasound and clinical findings do not match typical inflammation.

Sanity check: "Oscillatory movement" and "tiny hyperechoic foci" strongly point toward live motile structures, characteristic of filarial worms rather than stones or inflammation.

<final>Scrotal filariasis</final><|return|>
options: Scrotal filariasis | Dancing megasperm | Epididymitis

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 22-year-old female presented with dysphagia for 9 years and gradually increasing vulval swelling for 5 years. Clinical examination revealed a firm, non-tender, lobulated mass (~8x8 cm) involving bilateral labia with intact overlying skin. Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy showed a bulge in the mid and lower oesophagus with intact mucosa and luminal narrowing.
 Imaging Findings: MRI thorax showed a long segment bulky circumferential wall thickening of the lower thoracic oesophagus extending till the gastric cardia (Figure 1c), with T1 isointense (Figure 1a) and T2 mildly hyperintense appearance (Figure 1b) compared to the skeletal muscle. It shows mild homogeneous enhancement without necrotic areas (Figure 1d), a very smooth outline and a clear plane with the aorta, cardia and thoracic spine.
 
 MRI pelvis showed a large, lobulated, well-circumscribed mass (~9x7 cm) involving the clitoral region and bilateral labia majora. It appears T1 isointense (Figure 2b) and T2 hyperintense compared to that of skeletal muscle (Figures 2a and 2d), it shows diffusion restriction (Figures 2e and 2f) and a marked homogeneous contrast enhancement (Figure 2c). The mass was seen overhanging the urethral orifice and vaginal introitus without causing urinary outflow obstruction.
 
 Non-contrast PET-CT showed
 
 FDG-avid soft tissue lesions corresponding to the oesophageal wall thickening (SUV max = 9.2) (Figures 1e and 1f) and vulval mass (SUV max = 9.8) (Figures 2g and 2h). There were no calcifications, intralesional fat, or cystic areas within the lesions.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Clinical: Young female (22y) with 9 years of dysphagia and 5 years of progressive vulval swelling. Physical exam shows a firm, non-tender, lobulated mass involving bilateral labia (~8x8 cm).  
- Endoscopy: Bulge in mid to lower esophagus with luminal narrowing but intact mucosa.  
- MRI Thorax: Long segment thickening of lower thoracic esophagus to gastric cardia, T1 isointense, T2 mildly hyperintense, mild homogeneous enhancement, smooth contour, clear plane from aorta, cardia, spine.  
- MRI Pelvis: Lobulated, well-circumscribed vulval mass (~9x7 cm) T1 isointense, T2 hyperintense, diffusion restriction, marked homogeneous contrast enhancement. No calcification, intralesional fat or cyst. Overhangs urethra and vaginal introitus without urinary obstruction.  
- PET-CT: Both lesions FDG-avid (SUVmax ~9.2-9.8).  
- No mention of nodal disease or distant metastases.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Lymphoma  
Pros: Young age; bulky mass with homogeneous enhancement; involvement of mucosal layers with intact mucosa could be lymphoma; FDG avidity high; diffuse long segment esophageal involvement often seen in lymphoma; vulval lymphoma is rare but possible.  
Cons: Lymphoma usually homogeneous with no well-defined mass, but vulval lymphoma is rare; presence of a distinct lobulated mass rather than nodal involvement may argue against typical lymphoma; no mention of systemic B symptoms or nodes.

2) Sarcoma  
Pros: Lobulated mass with restricted diffusion and marked enhancement; can involve soft tissues including vulva; sarcoma can present as large soft tissue mass.  
Cons: Sarcomas often show necrosis, heterogeneity or calcifications (none seen); sarcomas of esophagus are extremely rare; smooth contour and clear plane to aorta and spine argue against invasive sarcoma; presence of FDG avidity but no necrosis or heterogeneity less typical for sarcoma.

3) Benign mesenchymal tumour  
Pros: Well-circumscribed; homogeneous enhancement; no necrosis or fat; benign mesenchymal tumors (e.g., leiomyoma) can occur in esophagus; vulval leiomyoma can present as a mass.  
Cons: Benign tumors usually less FDG avid (SUVmax ~2-5); multiple lesions and bulky esophageal thickening unlikely for benign; also, large diffuse involvement of esophagus with luminal narrowing less typical for benign.

4) Oesophageal-vulvar syndrome  
Pros: Classical clinical triad (young Asian female, dysphagia, vulvar swelling), typical imaging features with esophageal thickening and vulval mass; no nodal or metastatic spread (localised disease); FDG uptake moderate to high but can be due to inflammatory or hypermetabolic processes; pathology is unknown but malignant potential minimal; imaging findings (smooth outline, homogeneous enhancement, no necrosis or invasion) consistent; clinical history fits well.  
Cons: Rare syndrome but fits clinical and imaging features perfectly.

C) Ranking:  
1) Oesophageal-vulvar syndrome (perfect match to clinical and imaging features, pathognomonic radiology and clinical presentation).  
2) Lymphoma (young age and FDG avid bulky masses, but less typical for this presentation and rare site).  
3) Benign mesenchymal tumour (possible but FDG avidity and extensive esophageal involvement argue against primary benign tumor).  
4) Sarcoma (least likely given lack of necrosis, heterogeneity, and atypical location).  

Thus, best diagnosis given clinical and imaging correlation is Oesophageal-vulvar syndrome.  
</analysis>  

<final>Oesophageal-vulvar syndrome</final>
<|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 6-month-old infant presented with a history of on-and-off soft tissue swelling over the left cheek since 2 months (Figure 1). No history of trauma. Full-term normal delivery.
 Imaging Findings: Head and neck CT with bone reconstruction showed cortical irregularity with sclerosis, solid periosteal reaction, increase in cortical width and density, seen along the angle and ramus of the left hemimandible (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 On MRI, the corresponding area showed mild STIR hyperintensity (Figure 3) and T1 hypointensity (Figure 4). No evidence of any significant diffusion restriction was seen in this region (Figures 5a and 5b). Surrounding oedematous changes were seen in the left masticator muscles and infratemporal fossa. No focal discrete collection or solid component was seen.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 6-month-old infant presented with a history of on-and-off soft tissue swelling over the left cheek since 2 months. No history of trauma. Full-term normal delivery.
 Imaging Findings: Head and neck CT with bone reconstruction showed cortical irregularity with sclerosis, solid periosteal reaction, increase in cortical width and density, seen along the angle and ramus of the left hemimandible. On MRI, the corresponding area showed mild STIR hyperintensity and T1 hypointensity. No evidence of any significant diffusion restriction was seen in this region. Surrounding oedematous changes were seen in the left masticator muscles and infratemporal fossa. No focal discrete collection or solid component was seen.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Osteomyelitis, Battered baby syndrome, Hypervitaminosis A, Hyperphosphataemia, Ewing sarcoma, Scurvy, Infantile cortical hyperostosis (Caffey disease), Malignant infantile hyperpetrosis, Prolonged PGE1 infusion
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Age: 6-month-old infant with a 2-month history of on-and-off soft tissue swelling over the left cheek.  
- Imaging: CT head and neck with bone reconstruction shows cortical irregularity with sclerosis, solid periosteal reaction, increased cortical width and density along the angle and ramus of the left hemimandible.  
- MRI shows mild STIR hyperintensity and T1 hypointensity in the same area, with surrounding edematous changes in left masticator muscles and infratemporal fossa; no significant diffusion restriction.  
- No focal discrete collection or solid mass identified.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
- Osteomyelitis: Pros: Cortical irregularity, sclerosis, periosteal reaction, perimandibular soft tissue swelling, edema; Cons: No abscess or fluid collection, no diffusion restriction indicating abscess, clinical course intermittent rather than progressive, patient relatively stable, no fever or systemic signs mentioned.  
- Battered baby syndrome: Pros: Possible fractures in mandible; Cons: No fractures but periosteal reaction and cortical irregularity less typical; no trauma history; no other fractures described.  
- Hypervitaminosis A: Pros: Periosteal reaction can be seen; Cons: Typically bilateral findings; no description of skull involvement; no other systemic signs.  
- Hyperphosphataemia: Pros: Potentially bone changes; Cons: Rare in infancy, no metabolic labs given, no classic imaging pattern described.  
- Ewing sarcoma: Pros: Periosteal reaction and bone changes; Cons: Usually aggressive, often with periosteal layering ("onion skin" or "lamellated"/codman triangle), often intramedullary mass, and diffusion restriction. No solid tumor seen or diffusion restriction here.  
- Scurvy: Pros: Rare in infants; may cause cortical irregularity; Cons: Typically other features like subperiosteal hemorrhages, ground-glass appearance, ring epiphyses not described here. Lack of systemic signs.  
- Infantile cortical hyperostosis (Caffey disease): Pros: Classic involvement of mandible in infant, cortical thickening and sclerosis, periosteal reaction; consistent with periosteal reaction and soft tissue swelling; often self-limiting and appears by 6 months.  
- Malignant infantile hyperpetrosis: Rare, no characteristic imaging pattern described, no supporting clinical data.  
- Prolonged PGE1 infusion: Typically in neonates with congenital heart disease; no data supporting this in this case.

C) Ranking:  
1) Infantile cortical hyperostosis (Caffey disease) - most consistent with age, site (mandible), imaging findings (cortical thickening, sclerosis, periosteal reaction), and clinical history.  
2) Osteomyelitis - possible but less likely due to lack of abscess, no diffusion restriction or systemic signs.  
3) Ewing sarcoma - less likely; no solid mass, no aggressive features.  
4) Battered baby syndrome - unlikely due to lack of trauma and fracture pattern.  
5) Hypervitaminosis A - less likely; usually bilateral and diffuse cranial involvement.  
6) Scurvy - less likely; no classic radiographic signs.  
7) Hyperphosphataemia, Malignant infantile hyperpetrosis, Prolonged PGE1 infusion - very unlikely based on clinical and imaging data.

Thus, diagnosis: Infantile cortical hyperostosis (Caffey disease)  
<final>Infantile cortical hyperostosis (Caffey disease)</final><|return|>
options: Osteomyelitis | Battered baby syndrome | Hypervitaminosis A | Hyperphosphataemia | Ewing sarcoma | Scurvy | Infantile cortical hyperostosis (Caffey disease) | Malignant infantile hyperpetrosis | Prolonged PGE1 infusion

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-year-old male child was brought by his 34-year-old mother with complaints of abdominal pain and recurrent vomiting. The child was clinically stable, and the abdominal ultrasound was normal. However, we incidentally discovered that both the mother and the son had physical impairments in their bilateral upper and lower limbs.
 Imaging Findings: The child had a classical median cleft with rudimentary bilateral thumbs with loss of opposition movement (Figure 1). A frontal radiograph revealed a bilateral median cleft, and the right hand’s proximal phalanges of the middle and index fingers fused, resulting in syndactyly. An additional middle phalanx was observed in the right thumb. Bilaterally, the thumb and index finger were displaced toward each other (Figures 2a and 2b). The radiography of the child’s feet depicted the absence of three of his metatarsals (Figure 2c). The X-ray of the infant’s skull showed multiple convolutions, suggesting a copper-beaten appearance (Figure 3).
 
 Interestingly, his 34-year-old mother, too, suffered from a similar deformity. On clinical inspection, both her right and left hands showed the presence of only three fingers (Figure 4a). A hypoplastic index finger was seen in the left hand. Bilateral feet showed only two toes with possible syndactyly (Figure 4b).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-year-old male child was brought by his 34-year-old mother with complaints of abdominal pain and recurrent vomiting. The child was clinically stable, and the abdominal ultrasound was normal. However, we incidentally discovered that both the mother and the son had physical impairments in their bilateral upper and lower limbs.
 Imaging Findings: The child had a classical median cleft with rudimentary bilateral thumbs with loss of opposition movement. A frontal radiograph revealed a bilateral median cleft, and the right hand’s proximal phalanges of the middle and index fingers fused, resulting in syndactyly. An additional middle phalanx was observed in the right thumb. Bilaterally, the thumb and index finger were displaced toward each other. The radiography of the child’s feet depicted the absence of three of his metatarsals. The X-ray of the infant’s skull showed multiple convolutions, suggesting a copper-beaten appearance. Interestingly, his 34-year-old mother, too, suffered from a similar deformity. On clinical inspection, both her right and left hands showed the presence of only three fingers. A hypoplastic index finger was seen in the left hand. Bilateral feet showed only two toes with possible syndactyly.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Syndactyla, Roberts syndrome, Split hand/split foot malformation (SHFM), Ectrodactyly-ectodermal dysplasia-cleft lip and palate (EEC), Amniotic band sequence, Lacrimo-Auriculo-dento-digital syndrome (LADD)

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  

- A 2-year-old male child and his mother both have limb anomalies including median clefts of the hands, missing or rudimentary thumbs, syndactyly, and absent metatarsals in the feet.  
- Radiographs show median clefts on both hands, fusion of proximal phalanges (syndactyly), and an extra middle phalanx in the right thumb.  
- Feet show absence of three metatarsals in the child and only two toes in the mother with possible syndactyly.  
- Bilateral upper and lower limb deformities in both child and mother suggest a genetic disorder with autosomal recessive pattern or familial transmission.  
- The "copper-beaten" skull appearance indicates intracranial hypertension due to thickened sutures and grooves but may be incidental.  
- The mother and child both have median cleft hand and synpolydactyly.  
- Growth and psychomotor development appear normal, and there are no systemic manifestations described.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  

1) Syndactyly (syndactyla):  
   - Pros: Syndactyly is present in both child and mother.  
   - Cons: Syndactyly alone does not explain median clefts, absent metatarsals, or familial transmission pattern; it is usually an isolated finding or part of a syndrome rather than a syndrome itself.  
   Verdict: Incomplete explanation; fail.  

2) Roberts syndrome:  
   - Pros: Features like median clefts, syndactyly, limb malformations (especially the hand and foot anomalies), and craniosynostosis-related changes on skull radiographs could fit.  
   - Cons: Roberts syndrome usually includes growth retardation, prenatal or postnatal growth retardation, facial dysmorphisms, and severe limb defects. In this case, growth and psychomotor development are normal, and family history suggests a less severe phenotype without severe developmental delays.  
   Verdict: Fit but does not match clinical progression; pass.  

3) Split hand/split foot malformation (SHFM):  
   - Pros: Median clefts of the hands and missing toes/metatarsals are consistent. SHFM classically presents with longitudinal deficiencies of the central rays in limbs, leading to cleft hands and feet. It can be isolated or part of a syndrome but is consistent with described findings. Familial occurrence fits the mode of inheritance.  
   - Cons: Presence of extra middle phalanx may be uncommon but can be a variation in SHFM. No cranial findings usually linked but not exclusive.  
   Verdict: Strong candidate.  

4) Ectrodactyly-ectodermal dysplasia-cleft lip and palate (EEC):  
   - Pros: EEC includes ectrodactyly (split hand/foot), ectodermal dysplasia (e.g., hair, teeth, sweat gland anomalies), and orofacial clefting.  
   - Cons: No mention of ectodermal dysplasia features or orofacial clefts. Therefore less likely in this patient.  
   Verdict: Fail.  

5) Amniotic band sequence:  
   - Pros: Limb malformations, amputations can be caused by amniotic bands.  
   - Cons: Generally asymmetric, sporadic, and unilateral; bilateral symmetric findings and inheritance strongly argue against amniotic band syndrome.  
   Verdict: Fail.  

6) Lacrimo-Auriculo-dento-digital syndrome (LADD):  
   - Pros: Limb anomalies can be present.  
   - Cons: Classic LADD involves lacrimal anomalies, auricular malformations, and dental anomalies; no mention of such in this case.  
   Verdict: Fail.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  

1) Split hand/split foot malformation (SHFM) – best fit for major findings (median cleft hand, absent toes/metatarsals, familial transmission).  
2) Roberts syndrome – plausible due to median clefts and limb anomalies, but lack of severe growth retardation and normal development make it less likely.  
3) Syndactyly – inadequate explanation.  
4) EEC, Amniotic band, LADD – less likely due to lack of supporting features.  

Therefore, SHFM is the most accurate diagnosis according to the clinical and imaging findings described.  
</analysis>  
<final>Split hand/split foot malformation (SHFM)</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 42-year-old male presented to the emergency department with weakness and paraesthesia in the lower limbs, back pain, and fever. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis showed an increased white blood cell count (predominantly polymorphonuclear) and protein. He had a history of gonococcal urethritis and syphilis and a recent influenza B respiratory infection.
 Imaging Findings: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) shows a longitudinally extensive posterior extradural extramedullary collection from T2 to T9 of heterogeneous appearance with isointense and hyperintense areas in T1-weighted sequence (Figure 1) and high signal intensity on T2-weighted and STIR sequences (Figure 2). It causes a compressive effect on the spinal cord with anterior thecal sac displacement (Figure 3). There is increased STIR and T2 cord signal in the compressed segment, suggestive of compressive myelopathy (Figure 2). The contrast-enhanced T1-weighted sequence shows diffuse meningeal spinal enhancement (Figures 4, 5a and 5b). The magnetic field strength of the MRI scanner used was 3T.

The contrast-enhanced T1-weighted sequence shows diffuse meningeal spinal enhancement. The magnetic field strength of the MRI scanner used was 3T.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Epidural haematoma, Postoperative spinal seroma, Disc herniation, Spinal epidural abscess, Metastatic spinal disease
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 42-year-old male with acute neurological symptoms (lower limb weakness, paraesthesia, back pain, fever).
- CSF shows increased white blood cells (predominantly polymorphonuclear) and high protein, indicating infection/inflammation.
- Patient has a history of gonococcal urethritis and syphilis, with recent influenza B infection, suggesting a potential infectious or inflammatory etiology.
- MRI (3T) shows a longitudinally extensive posterior extradural extramedullary collection from T2 to T9, heterogeneous in appearance with isointense and hyperintense areas on T1, high signal intensity on T2 and STIR, causing compressive myelopathy.
- Contrast-enhanced T1 sequence shows diffuse meningeal spinal enhancement.

B) Candidate pass/fail:

1) Epidural haematoma
- Pros: Posterior extradural collection causing spinal cord compression; possible spinal cord signal changes.
- Cons: Typically acute bleed with T1 hyperintensity due to methemoglobin or subacute clot; no history or risk factors for bleeding; no mention of trauma or coagulopathy; CSF findings more consistent with infection/inflammation rather than haemorrhage.

2) Postoperative spinal seroma
- Pros: Posterior extradural collection could be seroma; compressive myelopathy.
- Cons: No history of surgery or invasive spinal procedure was mentioned; seromas are usually postoperative complications.

3) Disk herniation
- Pros: Compressive myelopathy; extramedullary lesion.
- Cons: Disk herniations are usually anterior or posterolateral, not posterior midline continuous collections; herniated discs typically have different MRI characteristics and are not longitudinally extensive spanning T2 to T9; no degenerative changes described; inflammatory CSF findings not typical.

4) Spinal epidural abscess
- Pros: Posterior extradural collection from T2 to T9 with heterogeneous signal and meningeal enhancement; high signal on T2 and STIR indicating edema/inflammation; CSF shows neutrophilic pleocytosis and high protein; fever and constitutional symptoms; known infection risk from gonococcal urethritis and syphilis.
- Cons: None significant; clinical and imaging findings fit well.

5) Metastatic spinal disease
- Pros: Posterior extradural lesion could be metastatic; compression myelopathy possible.
- Cons: Usually involves bone lesions, vertebral bodies, not large extradural extramedullary collections; no mention of primary cancer; CSF findings and infection signs inconsistent.

C) Ranking:
1) Spinal epidural abscess (best fit with clinical and imaging findings; infection confirmed by CSF; MRI with typical longitudinal epidural enhancement and compression).
2) Epidural haematoma (less likely due to lack of trauma and infection signs).
3) Postoperative spinal seroma (no surgical history).
4) Disk herniation (unlikely long continuity and posterior extradural location).
5) Metastatic spinal disease (no cancer history or bone involvement).

<final> Spinal epidural abscess </final><|return|>
options: Epidural haematoma | Postoperative spinal seroma | Disc herniation | Spinal epidural abscess | Metastatic spinal disease

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-day-old female neonate born through normal vaginal delivery to non-consanguineous marriage was found to have dark pigmentation in the groin, perineum, lower back, upper and lower extremities, including hands, with hair growth in these areas (Figure 1). No other abnormalities on clinical examination and blood workup. This finding raised suspicion of phacomatosis, prompting a brain MRI to assess for neuroparenchymal abnormalities.
 Imaging Findings: Multifocal punctate T1 hyperintense foci scattered in bilateral cerebral cortices, left amygdala and left cerebellar hemisphere (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). These did not show restricted diffusion or blooming on SWI. No signal changes were seen on T2 (Figure 3). No leptomeningeal T1 hyperintensities were seen. Post-contrast images showed no leptomeningeal or neuroparenchymal enhancement.

A) Key findings:

1) Subacute haemorrhage:
   Pros: T1 hyperintense lesions can represent subacute haemorrhage due to methemoglobin.
   Cons: Lesions are multifocal bilateral cortical and cerebellar, without restricted diffusion or hemosiderin blooming typical of subacute haemorrhage. Clinical context does not align with hemorrhage (no risk factors or symptoms). T2 signal unchanged, which is unusual for haemorrhage stages.

   Pros: Melanotic skin lesions with hair growth suggest congenital melanosis. T1 hyperintense foci in cortex and cerebellum fit known neurocutaneous melanosis imaging pattern (melanin signal on T1). Absence of diffusion restriction or blooming supports melanotic rather than haemorrhagic lesions. No leptomeningeal signs, which is common as leptomeningeal involvement is variable.
   Cons: None significant. The imaging and clinical findings are classic.

3) Subacute ischaemia:
   Pros: Could cause T1 hyperintense lesions.
   Cons: Clinical context is neonate with pigmented skin lesions, no neurological deficits or relevant events. No diffusion restriction expected in subacute phase? Typically more T2 changes. Imaging findings not typical for ischaemia. Clinical picture does not support.

C) Ranking:
1) Neurocutaneous melanosis (matches clinical and imaging findings perfectly).
2) Subacute haemorrhage (less likely due to typical imaging features and clinical scenario).
3) Subacute ischaemia (least likely).

Pathognomonic imaging (melanotic lesions in specific anatomic sites in neonate with skin findings) support neurocutaneous melanosis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 14-year-old boy presented with a chronic history of rhinosinusitis and productive cough for years. There were no associated symptoms of haemoptysis, fever, or weight loss. On examination, he had mild basal crepitations. Otherwise, no dysmorphic features or dextrocardia. No palpable organomegaly or lymphadenopathy. History of paternal aunt infertility was reported.
 Imaging Findings: Initial chest radiograph showed mild right lower zone faint opacification with no dextrocardia (Figure 1). The high-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) revealed mild bilateral lower lobe predominant bronchiectasis with bronchial wall thickening and mucoid impaction as well as centrilobular nodules (Figures 2a and 2b). The rest of the lung parenchyma was well aerated. No situs inversus or dextrocardia was seen. No mediastinal lymphadenopathy.
 
 Paranasal sinuses (PNS) CT revealed near complete opacification of the paranasal sinuses with frothy secretions as well as associated pharyngitis and left-sided otomastoiditis (Figure 3).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 14-year-old boy presented with a chronic history of rhinosinusitis and productive cough for years. There were no associated symptoms of haemoptysis, fever, or weight loss. On examination, he had mild basal crepitations. Otherwise, no dysmorphic features or dextrocardia. No palpable organomegaly or lymphadenopathy. History of paternal aunt infertility was reported.
 
 Imaging Findings: Initial chest radiograph showed mild right lower zone faint opacification with no dextrocardia. The high-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) revealed mild bilateral lower lobe predominant bronchiectasis with bronchial wall thickening and mucoid impaction as well as centrilobular nodules. The rest of the lung parenchyma was well aerated. No situs inversus or dextrocardia was seen. No mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Paranasal sinuses (PNS) CT revealed near complete opacification of the paranasal sinuses with frothy secretions as well as associated pharyngitis and left-sided otomastoiditis.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Cystic fibrosis, Primary ciliary dyskinesia, Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA), Williams–Campbell syndrome
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 14-year-old boy with chronic rhinosinusitis and productive cough for years  
- Mild basal crepitations on exam, no fever, no weight loss, no dysmorphic features, no situs inversus or dextrocardia  
- Paternal aunt infertility noted (possible autosomal recessive familial condition)  
- Chest X-ray: mild right lower zone faint opacification, no dextrocardia  
- HRCT: mild bilateral lower lobe predominant bronchiectasis, bronchial wall thickening, mucoid impaction, centrilobular nodules, otherwise normal lung parenchyma  
- No mediastinal lymphadenopathy  
- Paranasal sinus CT: near total opacification of sinuses with frothy secretions, pharyngitis, left otomastoiditis  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1) Cystic fibrosis  
- Pros: Chronic productive cough and recurrent sinopulmonary infections; bronchiectasis and mucus plugging typical in CF; sinus disease with frothy secretions fits sinus opacification in CF.  
- Cons: No dextrocardia needed for CF; age is typical but absence of typical CF features like failure to thrive or sweat test data; unilateral right lower lobe involvement is mild rather than typical diffuse or upper lobe predominant disease. Also CF often has pancreatic involvement or other systemic manifestations not reported here. No dysmorphic or cardiac anomalies.  

2) Primary ciliary dyskinesia (PCD)  
- Pros: Chronic rhinosinusitis, bronchiectasis (especially lower lobe), frequent otitis media/otitis media with effusion and sinusitis typical of PCD; infant or childhood onset common. Mucus stasis and mucoid impaction common; normal chest X-ray without dextrocardia (no situs inversus here).  
- Cons: Absence of dextrocardia does not exclude PCD (Kartagener syndrome is PCD with situs inversus, but PCD can occur without situs inversus). PCD usually has diffuse bronchiectasis, not only mild lower lobe predominant; age and chronic course fit. No associated infertility in the patient, but aunt infertility might indicate autosomal recessive inheritance.  

3) Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA)  
- Pros: Bronchiectasis and mucus plugging seen in ABPA; bilateral involvement.  
- Cons: No mention of asthma, eosinophilia, elevated IgE or Aspergillus sensitization history. Sinus disease and otitis media less typical. ABPA tends to cause upper lobe bronchiectasis. Chronic sinus disease less typical in ABPA alone.  

4) Williams–Campbell syndrome  
- Pros: Bronchiectasis mainly affecting the peripheral airways, usually bronchioles, not the major conducting bronchi.  
- Cons: Rare congenital condition characterized by deficiency of cartilage in bronchioles causing bronchiectasis, usually more diffuse and severe. No specific sinus disease or ear involvement typical. No cardiopulmonary complications or associated abnormalities mentioned.  
  
C) Ranking:  
1) Primary ciliary dyskinesia – best fit given chronic sinusitis, otitis media, lower lobe bronchiectasis, mucus plugging; absence of situs inversus does not exclude PCD.  
2) Cystic fibrosis – plausible given bronchiectasis and sinus disease, but less specific findings and no CF systemic symptoms reported.  
3) Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis – less likely given lack of asthma history or ABPA typical features.  
4) Williams–Campbell syndrome – unlikely due to lack of typical diffuse bronchiectasis, sinopulmonary infection pattern, and rare presentation.  

<final>Primary ciliary dyskinesia</final><|return|>
options: Cystic fibrosis | Primary ciliary dyskinesia | Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA) | Williams–Campbell syndrome

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A previously well 12-year-old male presented to the accident and emergency department of a regional paediatric hospital with a 2-week history of worsening bilateral lower limb paraesthesia in the form of loss of fine touch, vibration and proprioception from the level of T5 distally, signifying mass effect on the dorsal columns with sparing of pain and temperature sensation. He also had reduced motor function of the lower limbs with clonus and brisk reflexes.
 Imaging Findings: MRI brain was normal. MRI of the whole spine (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, 1d, and 1e) demonstrated a large solitary solid cystic lesion, centred over the spinous process and the lamina of T5 bilaterally, more extensive on the right. The solid lesion is iso/mildly hypointense to bone, with areas of T1 and T2 hypointense signal, suggestive of calcifications. The solid component measured 3.4 x 3.7 x 4.4 cm (transverse x anteroposterior x craniocaudal) and demonstrated avid enhancement. The mass is expansile with cortical expansion and trabecular replacement. It extends into the spinal canal, causing severe canal stenosis and cord compression. There was evidence of bone marrow oedema within the body of T5. No other intra or extramedullary lesions were identified.
 
 A follow-up CT thorax, abdomen and pelvis (Figures 2a and 2b) with bony reformats demonstrated an expansile lytic lesion with cortical expansion involving the posterior elements of T5. There was no bony destruction. There are areas of calcification seen within the internal osseous matrix. No other bony lesions were identified, and no abnormality of note was found in the thorax, abdomen or pelvis.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Age: 12-year-old male.  
- Clinical: Progressive bilateral lower limb sensory deficits (loss of fine touch, vibration, proprioception) with motor signs (reduced motor function, clonus, brisk reflexes), indicating dorsal column and corticospinal tract involvement.  
- MRI: Large solitary solid and cystic lesion centered around T5 spinous process and lamina, expansile nature with cortical expansion and trabecular replacement, avidly enhancing solid component, mixed iso/mild hypointensity relative to bone with T1 and T2 hypointense areas suggesting calcifications.  
- CT: Expansile lytic lesion with cortical expansion, no bony destruction, internal matrix calcifications.  
- No other lesions in spine or rest of skeleton, normal MRI brain.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Osteoblastoma  
Pros: Commonly arises in posterior elements of spine (lamina, pedicles) in adolescents/young adults; expansile lytic lesion with cortical expansion and internal calcifications; MRI typically shows a heterogeneous lesion with solid and cystic components and avid enhancement.  

Cons: Usually presents with pain; though neurological deficits can occur with large lesions compressing spinal canal.  

2) Fibrous dysplasia  
Pros: Can involve spine posterior elements; expansile lesion with woven bone and fibrous tissue causing cortical thinning and matrix calcifications.  

Cons: Fibrous dysplasia lesions are typically non-enhancing or mildly enhancing and not usually causing severe canal stenosis and aggressive cord compression. No mention of typical “ground glass” matrix on CT. Also, neurological symptoms of this severity are atypical.  

3) Telangiectatic osteosarcoma  
Pros: Aggressive lesion with lytic, expansile components; can occur in young patients.  

Cons: Typically destructive bone lesion with cortical disruption, periosteal reaction, and large soft tissue mass; malignant features like aggressive periosteal reaction not described here. Also, no mention of malignant soft tissue component or necrosis. This case lacks destructive bone changes, favoring benign process.  

4) Giant cell tumour  
Pros: Commonly involves post-metaphyseal bone in young adults, can affect posterior vertebral elements; expansile lytic lesion with cortical thinning.  

Cons: Usually occurs in skeletally mature patients (18-40 years), not typically seen in 12-year-old. Also, less likely to have extensive internal calcifications. Less commonly causes severe canal stenosis without bone destruction.  

5) Ewing sarcoma  
Pros: Common malignant bone tumor in children and adolescents; involvement of spine possible.  

Cons: Typically destructive lesion with soft tissue mass, aggressive periosteal reaction ("onion skin") and bone destruction, but none is mentioned. Also, presence of calcifications less typical, and lesion is largely lytic with cortical expansion but no destructive features.  

C) Ranking:

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 15-year-old female presented with a two-year history of progressively enlarging, painful swellings on both sides of the neck, along with a recent onset of abdominal pain of one week’s duration. Examination revealed multiple palpable swellings in the axillary, cervical and inguinal regions as well as café-au-lait spots over the extremities and trunk.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasonography of the neck revealed multiple oval and spindle-shaped hypoechoic lesions in the bilateral posterior triangle (Figures 1a and 1b) and the anterior neck region. Similar subcutaneous lesions were noted in bilateral level 4 regions (Figures 1c and 1d), abutting carotid vessels.
 
 The contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CT) abdomen revealed heterogeneously enhancing oval-shaped lesions abutting the duodenojejunal flexure (Figures 2a and 2b) and a similar enhancing oblong lesion in the pelvis, posterior to the rectum at L5–S1 level (Figures 3a,3b, and 3c). Multiple hypodense, hypoenhancing nodules were imaged in the mesentery encasing the inferior mesenteric artery from its origin (Figures 4a and 4b), and extending into the pelvis and bilateral retroperitoneum. Similar nodular lesions were seen extending from the neural foramina of the lumbar spine, extending along the course of nerve roots into sciatica foramina and along the sciatic nerve (Figures 5a, 5b, and 5c). A few small nodules were also noted adjacent to bilateral femoral vessels (Figures 6a and 6b). Multiple variable-sized hypodense subcutaneous nodules were noted diffusely scattered in the abdominal wall.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 59-year-old male agricultural worker from a rural area, with a long history of barefoot fieldwork, presented with a long-standing history of swelling over the plantar surface of the left foot, now progressing to involve the dorsal surface and with a history of a discharging sinus. The patient denies any history of trauma.
 Imaging Findings: A clinical image of the plantar (Figure 1a) and dorsal surface (Figure 1b) of the left foot shows multiple soft and firm nodules that were nontender, along with no discharging sinus at present.
 
 X-ray of the left foot (Figure 2) and lateral views show soft-tissue density over the lateral aspect of the left foot with cortical remodelling of the diaphysis of the 4th metatarsal.
 
 Ultrasound shows multiple cystic lesions, with a few of them showing tiny echogenic grains in the dependent portion (dot in a circle) (arrow in Figure 3).
 
 On T2W sagittal (Figure 4a), soft tissue mass shows conglomerates of small (2–5 mm), rounded, hyperintense lesions with few of them showing central low-signal intensity dots and peripheral low-signal intensity rim around these hyperintense areas, representing a “dot-in-circle” sign. A few cystic areas are noted within this mass, which contains T2 hypointense foci—likely spores. On post-contrast T1 fat-saturated (FS) coronal image (Figure 4b), the involved region of soft tissue shows rim enhancement around the granulation tissue along with minimal enhancement of the central dot. There is sparing of the underlying bones and tendons. No evidence of marrow oedema noted.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A man in his mid-20s with a 2–3 week history of intermittent severe headache, photophobia, nausea and vomiting, presented with generalised seizures. Treatment for meningoencephalitis was given, and the patient improved. Two months later, he developed sudden bilateral lower limb weakness and pins and needles sensation below the waist.
 Imaging Findings: MRI showed diffuse cortical and subcortical white matter T2 hyperintensity involving the hippocampi and parahippocampal gyri bilaterally and cortical signal abnormality in the left superior frontal lobe (Figures 1a and 1b). Two weeks later, an MRI (not shown) showed near complete resolution of the abnormal signal intensities. No abnormalities were seen in the cervicothoracic spine.
 
 MRI whole spine two months later showed extensive increased signal of the central cord spanning from C2 to T8 vertebrae with cord expansion (Figures 2a and 2b), and areas of ring-enhancing foci within the cord at C2 (Figures 2c and d). There were also confluent and continuous lesions involving the medial aspect of the right temporal lobe, optic tract, corticospinal tract and hypothalamus. This extends into the right cerebral peduncle and the pyramids of the right pons (Figure 3).
PostDescription:

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Young male with subacute autoimmune-like neurologic presentation: severe headache, meningoencephalitis treated with improvement, followed by sudden bilateral lower limb weakness and sensory changes two months later.  
- Initial MRI: diffuse cortical/subcortical T2 hyperintensities in bilateral hippocampi and parahippocampal gyri and left superior frontal lobe with near complete resolution on follow-up MRI two weeks later.  
- Later MRI (two months after onset): extensive central cord signal abnormality from C2-T8 with cord expansion, ring-enhancing foci at C2; confluent lesions in right temporal lobe, optic tract, corticospinal tract, hypothalamus, right cerebral peduncle, and right pons pyramids.  
- Cervicothoracic spine unremarkable on earlier MRI, but extensive spinal cord involvement on later spine MRI.  
- Clinical course includes encephalitis, relapsing/transverse myelitis, and involvement of corticospinal tracts and optic tract.

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1. Multiple sclerosis (MS):  
- Pros: lesions in cerebral white matter, some partial involvement of spinal cord.  
- Cons: MS lesions usually do not show extensive central cord hyperintensity and expansion, nor ring-enhancing nodules as described; also the extensive longitudinal spinal cord lesion from C2-T8 is atypical for MS, which tends to involve short cord segments; clinical picture is not classic for MS.  

2. Neuro-Behçet’s disease:  
- Pros: can affect brain and spinal cord; multiple areas including brainstem and spinal cord.  
- Cons: No mention of systemic Behçet features (oral/genital ulcers, uveitis); lesion pattern is not typical; absence of vascular imaging or enhancement patterns typical for Behçët's; less likely given age and presentation.  

3. Aquaporin-4 IgG-positive neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorders (AQP4-NMOSD):  
- Pros: characteristic extensive, central cord lesions frequently spanning multiple segments (C2-T8), with extensive optic tract and brain involvement in specific areas (hypothalamus, periventricular brainstem); ring-enhancing cord lesions can be seen; clinical episode of encephalitis with seizures then myelitis fits NMOSD; this pattern of longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (LETM) is classic for NMOSD.  
- Cons: no optic neuritis clinically noted, but optic tract involvement imaging wise; however, optic involvement can be subclinical.  

4. Myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein antibody disease (MOGAD):  
- Pros: can cause extensive spinal cord lesions and encephalitis; can cause bilateral diffuse T2 lesions and encephalitis features.  
- Cons: MOGAD often shows patchy or asymmetric lesions, sometimes less extensive spinal cord involvement; ring-enhancing lesions less common. Optic nerve involvement in MOG is usually optic neuritis rather than optic tract lesions; this patient lacks clinical optic neuritis.  

5. Neurosarcoidosis:  
- Pros: can involve brain and spinal cord, sometimes enhancing lesions.  
- Cons: less likely due to lack of systemic sarcoidosis signs, biopsy evidence, pattern and location of lesions less typical; also clinical picture and acute myelitis pattern less typical; no mention of granulomatous lesions or leptomeningeal enhancement.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
- 1st: Aquaporin-4 IgG-positive neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorders (AQP4-NMOSD) - this is the best fit given the classic long extensive central cord lesion, ring-enhancing foci, brainstem and corticospinal tract lesions including hypothalamic and optic tract involvement, and clinical sequence of encephalitis followed by severe myelitis.  
- 2nd: Myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein antibody disease (MOGAD) - possible but imaging less typical.  
- 3rd: Multiple sclerosis - unlikely given the extensive central cord lesions.  
- 4th: Neuro-Behçet’s disease - no systemic features or typical enhancement pattern.  
- 5th: Neurosarcoidosis - less supported clinically and radiologically.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 11-year-old child presented with a pricking type of upper abdominal pain on and off for 6 months. No history of fever, vomiting, or loss of appetite. No previous history of similar complaints. On examination, tenderness is present over the epigastric and left lumbar region. Routine laboratory investigations were normal.
 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound, a relatively well-defined heteroechoic lesion with a hyperechoic rim is noted in the retropancreatic area, with a focal exophytic hypoechoic area in the anterior aspect of the lesion (Figure 1). No obvious internal vascularity on colour Doppler.
 
 On contrast-enhanced (CE) CT, a ~ 6.8 x 7 x 8.1 cm well-defined, smoothly marginated, heterodense soft tissue density lesion (predominantly fat component in the posterior aspect with few small solid components and calcific focus in the anterior aspect) is noted in the retropancreatic region (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
 
 Regarding relations and mass effect, the lesion, superiorly, abuts the coeliac trunk and inferiorly displaces the third part of the duodenum. Anteriorly, it displaces the body and tail of the pancreas, abuts the splenic artery and mildly compresses the splenic vein. Posteriorly, abuts the left renal vein. Medially, there is a widening of the duodenal C loop, and laterally, a displacement of adjacent bowel loops. There is no obvious evidence of infiltration with the structures mentioned above or vascular complications (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old woman presented with a 3-month history of progressive abdominal distension and vague pelvic discomfort. Physical examination revealed a palpable midline abdominal mass. Routine laboratory investigations were unremarkable. Abdominopelvic CT was performed for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: Unenhanced abdominopelvic CT demonstrated two large, well-defined, predominantly cystic pelvic masses (Figure 1).
 
 The right-sided lesion (22 × 15 × 19 cm), centred in the cul-de-sac and extending into the right iliac fossa, was intraperitoneal, displacing small-bowel loops superiorly and the uterus postero-leftward while preserving a fat plane with the right psoas muscle (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
 
 The left-sided mass (15 × 17 × 15 cm) occupied the left flank/iliac fossa, had a slightly irregular thick wall, and abutted the left psoas muscle, kidney, aorta and iliac vessels without an intervening fat plane, displacing ileo-jejunal loops anteromedially—features consistent with a retroperitoneal origin (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). Compression of the left ureter produced moderate pelvicalyceal and proximal ureteral dilatation (Figure 4). A morphologically normal left ovary was identified caudal to the mass (Figure 5), whereas the right ovary was obscured. No ascites, peritoneal implants or pathological lymph nodes were present.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 25-year-old male presented with headache and giddiness for 3–4 months. He previously experienced diplopia for one month, which resolved following a right-sided medium-pressure ventriculoperitoneal shunt surgery 1 year ago.
 Imaging Findings: MRI revealed a predominantly cystic lesion with minimal solid components, measuring approximately 24 × 33 × 73 mm, centred in the lower fourth ventricle. It appeared T1 hypointense (Figure 3a) and heterogeneously T2 hyperintense, with multiple fluid-intensity cystic areas (Figure 1a). The solid areas demonstrated minimal post-contrast enhancement (Figure 3b), while the cystic portions remained non-enhancing. Mild diffusion restriction on diffusion weighted imaging–apparent diffusion coefficient (DWI–ADC) sequences (Figures 4a and 4b), and blooming foci were noted on fast field echo sequences, with corresponding PHASE hyperintensities, suggesting haemosiderin deposition (Figures 5a and 5b). CT screening showed tiny calcific foci.
 
 The lesion caused anterior displacement of the posterior pons (Figure 1a), medulla, and upper cervical cord, and abutted the cerebellar hemispheres, vermis, and both middle and inferior cerebellar peduncles. Posteroinferiorly, it extended into the cisterna magna via the foramen of Magendie and into the spinal canal up to 28 mm below McRae’s line. Lateral extension into both cerebellopontine angles was noted via the foramina of Luschka, with associated mild upstream hydrocephalus (Figures 1b and 2a).

A) Key findings:  
- Young adult male with posterior fossa cystic lesion measuring ~2.4 x 3.3 x 7.3 cm.  
- Centrally located in the lower fourth ventricle, extending through foramen of Magendie and foramina of Luschka into cisterna magna and spinal canal. This precise location strongly suggests involvement of the midline posterior fossa structures.  
- Predominantly cystic lesion with minimal solid components; cystic areas demonstrate T1 hypointensity and heterogeneous T2 hyperintensity.  
- Minimal enhancement of solid portions; cystic portions non-enhancing.  
- Diffusion restriction is mild (which is not typical for hypercellular malignancies like medulloblastoma).  
- Presence of blooming on GRE sequences indicating hemosiderin deposition; tiny calcific foci on CT.  
- Anterior displacement of posterior pons, medulla, and upper cervical cord due to lesion mass effect. Mild upstream hydrocele.  
- The mass extends into both cerebellopontine angles through foramina of Luschka, suggesting involvement of the floor of the fourth ventricle and periventricular extension.

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  

1) Pilocytic astrocytoma:  
- Pros: Predilection for posterior fossa in children and young adults; cystic component with solid mural nodule is typical, but commonly located in cerebellar hemispheres.  
- Cons: Typically solid nodule with robust enhancement; lesion does not classically involve the fourth ventricle extensively, nor extend through foramina of Luschka or Magendie. Also, often shows prominent enhancement. The cystic component with minimal solid part and mild diffusion restriction does not fit well.  

2) Ependymoma:  
- Pros: Commonly arises from the fourth ventricle in children and young adults. Frequently demonstrates cystic areas, minimal enhancement of solid component, hemosiderin deposition due to hemorrhage, and can extend through foramina of Luschka and Magendie causing subarachnoid spread and hydrocephalus.  
- Cons: Usually there is more prominent diffusion restriction since these are intermediate/high grade; however, ependymomas show variable diffusion. Mild diffusion restriction does not exclude ependymoma.  

3) Dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumour (DNET):  
- Pros: Cortical-based tumor, usually supratentorial, causing seizures in young adults. Rarely located in the posterior fossa or fourth ventricle.  
- Cons: The lesion is in the lower fourth ventricle, which is atypical for DNET. Its cystic nature and hemosiderin is not typical.  

4) Rosette-forming glioneuronal tumour (RGNT):  
- Pros: Usually located in fourth ventricle, midline posterior fossa in young adults. Typically a mixed solid-cystic tumor with minimal enhancement, low to intermediate grade, showing hemosiderin deposition due to hemorrhage is known. May extend into subarachnoid spaces.  
- Cons: The described lesion matches typical location and features for RGNT. Minimal enhancement and mild diffusion restriction fit low-grade nature.  

5) Ganglioglioma:  
- Pros: Can occur in brainstem or cerebellar region; mixed cystic-solid appearance. Usually demonstrates vivid enhancement of solid nodule.  
- Cons: Typically occurs supratentorially or in brainstem; involvement of fourth ventricle with extension patterns as described is less common. Moreover, the minimal enhancement argues against typical ganglioglioma.  

6) Medulloblastoma:  
- Pros: Common infratentorial tumor in children and young adults with high cellularity, causing restricted diffusion, solid and can have cystic components.  
- Cons: Typically solid and highly enhancing, with significant diffusion restriction. The described mild restriction and predominantly cystic nature argue against medulloblastoma. Also, extension pattern via foramina of Luschka and Magendie is less typical.

2) Ependymoma - second best: typical location and features (fourth ventricle, hemosiderin), but usually more solid enhancement and higher diffusion restriction.  
3) Ganglioglioma - less likely due to atypical location and less enhancement.  
4) Pilocytic astrocytoma - site and enhancement pattern don't match well.  
5) DNET - location atypical.  
6) Medulloblastoma - enhancement and diffusion pattern not supportive.

Final check: RGNT matches precise location and imaging characteristics best.  

</analysis>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 43-year-old female patient presented to the emergency department with acute cholecystitis. During imaging work-up, an incidental iliopsoas mass was detected on CT. MRI was subsequently performed for further characterisation.
 Imaging Findings: CT shows a well-defined, complex iliopsoas mass containing fat and soft-tissue components, demonstrating heterogeneous enhancement and prominent internal vessels (Figures 1a and 1b).
 
 MRI reveals an encapsulated lesion with high T1 signal intensity, slightly lower than subcutaneous fat, and heterogeneously high T2 signal intensity, with only partial fat suppression (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 2d, 2e, and 2f). It exhibits relatively homogeneous hypervascular enhancement with curvilinear branching septations, with no restricted diffusion.
PostDescription:

MRI reveals an encapsulated lesion with high T1 signal intensity, slightly lower than subcutaneous fat, and heterogeneously high T2 signal intensity, with only partial fat suppression. It exhibits relatively homogeneous hypervascular enhancement with curvilinear branching septations, with no restricted diffusion.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Lipoma, Liposarcoma, Angiomyolipoma, Hibernoma, Myelolipoma, Fat necrosis
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Well-defined, complex lesion in the iliopsoas region containing both fat and soft tissue components.  
- Heterogeneous enhancement and prominent internal vessels on CT.  
- MRI shows high T1 signal intensity (slightly lower than subcutaneous fat) and heterogeneously high T2 signal intensity, with only partial fat suppression.  
- Relatively homogeneous hypervascular enhancement with curvilinear branching septations.  
- No restricted diffusion on MRI.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1) Lipoma  
   - Pros: Fat-containing lesion, usually well-defined, high T1 signal.  
   - Cons: Typically does not show prominent soft tissue components with enhancement, no prominent internal vessels or septations, usually homogeneous fat signal without partial suppression, and no hypervascular enhancement.  

2) Liposarcoma  
   - Pros: Can be complex with fat and soft tissue components, heterogeneous enhancement, internal vessels, and septations. Usually hypervascular and may show enhancement.  
   - Cons: Liposarcoma often has invasive margins or infiltrative growth patterns and may show diffusion restriction; no mention of such features. However, location in the iliopsoas is unusual but possible.  

3) Angiomyolipoma  
   - Pros: Consists of fat, smooth muscle, and blood vessels; hypervascular with prominent internal vessels; homogeneous enhancement; well-defined; commonly in kidney but can appear elsewhere.  
   - Cons: Usually reported in kidney/adrenal; iliopsoas location less common but possible.  

4) Hibernoma  
   - Pros: Fatty tumor with high T1 signal (slightly less than subcutaneous fat), hypermetabolic tissue; may contain vessels; relatively homogenous enhancement.  
   - Cons: Typically uniform fat signal with complete fat suppression on fat-saturated sequences; here only partial fat suppression suggests other components; usually not prominently vascular.  

5) Myelolipoma  
   - Pros: Well-defined mass with fat and soft tissue elements (myeloid tissue); occurs commonly in adrenal region but can be ectopic in retroperitoneum, possibly iliopsoas region; internal vessels and enhancement possible.  
   - Cons: Typically does not show hypervascularity with prominent branching vessels; MRI features may include heterogeneous appearance but usually no significant hypervascularity.  

6) Fat necrosis  
   - Pros: Contains fat and soft tissue components, may have irregular enhancement and vessels.  
   - Cons: Usually not well-defined, often presents with inflammatory changes and pain; lesion appears more infiltrative and less well-defined than described; no mention of inflammatory signs.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Angiomyolipoma: fits well with mixed fat and soft-tissue components, prominent internal vessels, and hypervascular enhancement. Although uncommon in the iliopsoas, ectopic angiomyolipoma can occur.  
2) Liposarcoma: also fits the heterogeneous fat and soft tissues with hypervascularity, but no diffusion restriction and well-defined margins argue slightly against it.  
3) Myelolipoma: fits well-defined lesion with fat and myeloid components, but lack of significant vessels and hypervascularity make it less likely.  
4) Hibernoma: incomplete fat suppression and hypervascularity less typical.  
5) Lipoma: hypervascularity and soft tissue components not typical.  
6) Fat necrosis: less likely due to well-defined encapsulated lesion with no inflammatory signs.  

Overall, angiomyolipoma, while rare in this location, best matches the described MRI and CT characteristics (well-defined lesion with fat and soft tissue, heterogeneous hypervascular enhancement with branching vessels, and no diffusion restriction). Liposarcoma is a possible alternative but lacks other invasive features and diffusion restriction.  
</analysis><final>Angiomyolipoma</final><|return|>

Clinical History: A 23-year-old woman presented with two weeks of abdominal distension, pelvic pain and urinary frequency. Emergency thoraco-abdominal computed tomography (CT) was performed, followed shortly by dedicated pelvic MRI for loco-regional characterisation.
 Imaging Findings: Initial contrast-enhanced pelvic CT (Figures 1a and 1b) demonstrated a 17 × 16 × 12 cm, predominantly solid, left retro-uterine mass with heterogeneous enhancement on the left ovary, anterior displacement of the uterus, and compression of both distal ureters, resulting in moderate bilateral hydroureteronephrosis. No macroscopic fat, calcification or pathological lymph-node enlargement was identified.
 
 Subsequent pelvic MRI (Figures 2a and 2b) showed mainly solid tissue with intermediate T2 signal (Figures 2a and 2b) and avid post-gadolinium enhancement (Figure 3), containing scattered cystic-necrotic foci. Diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) showed marked hyperintensity of the solid components with low ADC values, consistent with high cellularity (Figures 4a and 4b). The tumour–uterus interface was smooth, and the right ovary appeared normal, displaced anteriorly. Ascites was minimal and confined to the pouch of Douglas.
 
 No peritoneal implants or pathological lymphadenopathy were identified on either CT or MRI.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 11-year-old premenarche girl presented with a 3-week history of intermittent hypogastric pain, not being able to sleep for more than two hours at a time for the last 5 days. Clinical examination revealed a palpable pelvic mass and suprapubic tenderness. The patient had no other relevant findings on physical examination. Blood and urine tests were all within normal limits.
 Imaging Findings: A transabdominal pelvic ultrasound was performed and revealed a significant distension of the vagina, measuring 6.8 x 8.3 x 15.3 cm, with echogenic material occupying its lumen (approximately 450 ml). The distension extended to the uterus, which showed hyperechogenic material inside its lumen. Both annexes were of normal appearance. There was no evidence of acute appendicitis, hydronephrosis or other significant findings.

A) Key Findings:  
- Patient: 11-year-old premenarche girl with intermittent hypogastric pain and palpable pelvic mass.  
- Imaging: Pelvic ultrasound shows significant distension of the vagina (6.8 x 8.3 x 15.3 cm) containing echogenic material (~450 ml). The distended vagina extends into the uterus, which also shows hyperechogenic material inside its lumen. Both fallopian tubes (annexes) appear normal. No acute appendicitis, hydronephrosis, or other significant findings.  
- Clinical: No abnormalities in blood or urine tests.  

B) Candidate Pass/Fail with pros-cons:  
1) Haematometrocolpos  
 Pros: Haematometrocolpos is characterized by accumulation of blood in both the vagina (hematocolpos) and uterus (haematometra), which matches the imaging findings of distended vagina and uterus both filled with hyperechoic material (blood). The presentation in a premenarchal girl is typical for this condition.  
 Cons: None significant. The combination of vaginal distension and uterine involvement aligns well with haematometrocolpos.  

2) Haematocolpos  
 Pros: Haematocolpos involves blood accumulation in the vagina only, which is part of the findings.  
 Cons: Imaging also shows blood in the uterus (haematometra), expanding the diagnosis beyond haematocolpos alone. Haematocolpos typically does not involve the uterus. The involvement of both vagina and uterus suggests haematometrocolpos rather than isolated haematocolpos.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Haematometrocolpos - matches imaging findings of blood distension of both vagina and uterus.  
2) Haematocolpos - only includes vagina; does not include uterine involvement seen in imaging.  
Thus, haematometrocolpos is the best fit.  
</analysis><final> Haematometrocolpos </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A previously healthy 3-year-old boy presented with recurrent atraumatic pain and refusal to walk for several weeks. The right proximal lower leg was swollen, and mobilisation was painful. Laboratory analysis at admission showed increased white blood cell count of 12.6 x 10³/mm³ and C-reactive protein of 20.2 mg/l.
 Imaging Findings: Radiography showed an irregularly moth-eaten mixed osteolytic-sclerotic lesion with a wide zone of transition in the proximal tibial diaphysis. The cortex was permeated with a transverse pathological fracture, interrupted lamellar periosteal reaction, and soft tissue swelling (Figures 1a and 1b).
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) confirmed extensive bone marrow replacement in the proximal tibia with adjacent soft tissue mass showing heterogeneous contrast enhancement, cortical permeation, bone destruction with focal cortical breakthrough (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d). Bone marrow changes were hypointense on T1-weighted images (WI) and heterogeneous hyperintense on STIR and fat-suppressed PD images.
 
 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose positron emission tomography–computed tomography (FDG PET–CT) scan showed markedly increased uptake in the proximal right tibia, adjacent soft tissue, and right popliteal, inguinal, iliac and retroperitoneal lymph nodes (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). Other distant metastases were absent.
